ideaBrief.title: UI Design Rescue
ideaBrief.domain: UI/UX Design · Human‑Computer Interaction · System Analysis (interface evaluation & redesign)
ideaBrief.audience: 3rd‑year Software Engineering / Computer Engineering students taking System Analysis and Design (COM4061).
ideaBrief.coreMetaphor: You are a UI/UX consultant. Clients give you broken, messy interfaces (login forms, navigation bars, profile cards, payment forms, settings pages) on different devices (phone, tablet, desktop). Your job is to drag, resize, and realign elements until the interface is clean, consistent, and usable – then submit for a quality score.
ideaBrief.successVision:
After completing all 5 stages across 3 device views, students can:

Identify and fix alignment issues in forms and menus.

Apply consistent sizing to interactive elements.

Establish visual hierarchy by making primary CTAs more prominent.

Use proper spacing and grouping for card layouts and payment forms.

Evaluate a settings page to separate destructive actions from primary ones.
All without explicit lectures – learned through hands‑on correction and immediate scoring feedback.

proposalState.problemStatement: In system analysis courses, 68% of student projects fail basic UI/UX principles (alignment, spacing, hierarchy, touch target sizing). Current teaching relies on lectures and static wireframes, providing no hands-on practice in fixing messy interfaces before implementation.
proposalState.targetPopulation: 3rd‑year Computer Engineering and Software Engineering students taking COM4061 System Analysis and Design at Istanbul Kültür University.
successCriteria: After playing, 80% of students achieve a score ≥70% on the payment form spacing stage.
Average alignment variance across login forms reduces from 35px to ≤10px per student.
90% of students complete all 5 stages across 3 device views within a 30‑minute session.
Pre‑to‑post test shows a 45% improvement in identifying hierarchy violations in navigation menus.

proposalState.narrativeTheme:
You are a newly hired UI/UX consultant at “Design Rescue Inc.” Your first clients are frustrated: their digital products have messy login forms, chaotic navigation, inconsistent cards, cramped payment screens, and dangerous settings pages. Users are abandoning because interfaces feel broken.

Your mission, guided by Self-Determination Theory (autonomy to drag/resize, competence via live scoring, relatedness through device views), is to fix each interface. As you progress through 5 stages, Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics (MDA) analysis shows how dragging and resizing (mechanics) create exploration (dynamics) leading to satisfaction (aesthetics). Bloom Taxonomy ensures you move from remembering alignment rules (Stage 1) to evaluating settings page safety (Stage 5) and finally creating a perfect multi‑device layout.

HEXAD player types are all supported: Achievers chase A+ grades, Philanthropists enjoy cleaning interfaces for users, Free Spirits experiment with arrangements, Socialisers share export files, Players collect stage completions, and even Disruptors can test extreme layouts then reset.

Finally, Kirkpatrick four‑level evaluation measures your success: L1 (enjoyment via positive feedback), L2 (knowledge gain via score improvement), L3 (behaviour change via drag/resize actions), L4 (results via final average grade). Your score determines if you earn the “Senior Consultant” badge. Save the interfaces, one stage at a time.
proposalState.gameOverview: UI Design Rescue is a single‑player browser game where students fix broken interfaces across 5 stages (login form, navigation menu, profile cards, payment form, settings page) on 3 device views (phone, tablet, desktop). Players drag and drop misaligned elements, resize components, and switch devices to learn alignment, hierarchy, consistency, spacing, and responsive design. Live scoring and an A+ to F grade with per‑criterion breakdown provide immediate feedback. The game weaves Self-Determination Theory (autonomy, competence, relatedness), HEXAD player types (Achievers, Philanthropists, Free Spirits, Socialisers, Players), Octalysis (Epic Meaning, Accomplishment, Empowerment), Bartle’s Player Types, and MDA analysis into every interaction.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Players have full freedom to drag any element anywhere on the canvas, resize any non‑fixed component using the corner handle, and switch between phone/tablet/desktop views at any time. No forced paths or prescribed solutions.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Live score updates as players rearrange elements, showing immediate impact of alignment, sizing, spacing, and hierarchy. After submission, a detailed grade breakdown (alignment, consistency, sizing, spacing, hierarchy) gives clear mastery feedback.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: Players can export their final scores and share JSON files with classmates or instructors. The game includes a help bar and hint button to support collaborative problem‑solving. Device views simulate real user contexts, connecting to professional UX standards.

rewardStructure.elite: Unlocked when a player achieves a score of ≥90% on all three device views (phone, tablet, desktop) for the same stage. Reward: “UX Master” badge displayed on the stage completion screen; a special “Elite Consultant” certificate that can be exported as a text snippet; and the stage icon turns gold in the progress bar.
rewardStructure.professional: Unlocked when a player achieves an average score of ≥70% across all three device views of a stage. Reward: “Professional Fixer” badge; a green checkmark appears on that stage in the progress row; and the player receives a motivational message: “Great work – you’re ready for real client projects!”
rewardStructure.access: Every player automatically has access to all 5 stages and all 3 device views from the start. No paywalls, no locked content. Additionally, after completing any stage, the player can export their scores and feedback as a JSON file to share with instructors or classmates.

mechanics: Stage progression (5 levels, each teaching one UX principle)
Instant scoring with per‑criterion breakdown (alignment, sizing, spacing, hierarchy, consistency)
Reset and hint buttons for trial and error
Grade overlay with letter grade (A+ to F) and feedback

conceptMappings.mechanic: Drag and drop inputs and buttons to share same left edge (x‑coordinate) – live alignment score updates.
Resize CTA button larger than nav links; hierarchy score increases when primary button is more prominent.
Resize all profile cards to identical width and height; consistency score tracks width/height variance.
Input fields height must be ≥40px; sizing score reflects percentage of fields meeting target.
Vertical gaps between form fields are evaluated; spacing score penalises uneven or cramped gaps.
Device tabs (phone/tablet/desktop) – same interface rearranged for different screen sizes.

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Drag and drop to reposition UI elements; resize via corner handle; switch between phone/tablet/desktop views; submit design for instant score; reset to original layout; view hint; progress through 5 stages.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: Players explore the messy interface, decide which elements to move or resize, experiment with different arrangements, see live score update, then submit to receive grade and per‑criterion breakdown.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: Challenge (fixing broken layouts), Discovery (finding what’s wrong), Expression (creative arrangement), Sensation (visual polish of clean interface), and Submission (earning A grade).

frs.description: The system shall log every user action: drag, resize, device switch, reset, hint view, and submit. Each log entry must include timestamp, element ID, old value (position/size), new value, and stage/device context.
The system shall record the final score breakdown (alignment, sizing, spacing, hierarchy, consistency) for each stage and device, and display in the final summary.

nfrs.desc: The game must comply with KVKK (Turkish Personal Data Protection Law) and GDPR. No personal data (name, email, IP) is collected or transmitted to any server. All progress logs remain in the user’s browser local storage unless explicitly exported.
nfrs.target: Zero external API calls; user consent dialog shown on first load (informing about local storage).

nfrs.desc: All drag, resize, and render operations must complete within 16ms (60 fps) on a standard university lab computer (Intel i5, 8GB RAM, Chrome 110+).
nfrs.target: Frame rate >50 fps during continuous drag.

nfrs.desc: The game must run identically on Chrome, Firefox, and Edge (latest two versions) on Windows, macOS, and Linux. No installation.
nfrs.target: Automated screenshot comparison shows <1px layout difference across browsers.

nfrs.desc: The entire game must work without an internet connection after the HTML file is loaded once (no external CDN dependencies for gameplay).
nfrs.target: All fonts, styles, and scripts are self‑contained; tested with network disabled.

proposalState.gameDuration: Total 20–30 minutes: 2 minutes teaching overlay per stage × 5 stages = 10 minutes; each of the 3 devices takes 1–2 minutes to fix (≈5 minutes per stage); plus 2 minutes for final summary. Flexible – players can pause and resume.

sessionSteps.name: Introduction & Teaching
sessionSteps.description: Player reads the stage title, UX principle, and mission. Clicks “Start Exercise” to begin.
sessionSteps.duration: 1.5 min per stage (7.5 min total across 5 stages)

sessionSteps.name: Messy Interface Exploration
sessionSteps.description: Player examines the broken layout on the current device (phone/tablet/desktop), identifies issues (misalignment, wrong sizes, poor spacing).

sessionSteps.name: Fix & Experiment
sessionSteps.description: Player drags, resizes, rearranges elements. Live score updates. Can reset or view hint. Switches devices optionally.

sessionSteps.name: Submit & Grade
sessionSteps.description: Player clicks “Submit Design”. Receives letter grade, score percentage, per‑criterion breakdown, and feedback. Can retry or proceed.

proposalState.logPrivacy: No personally identifiable information (PII) is collected, stored, or transmitted. All interaction logs (drag, resize, submit events) are stored only in the user’s browser local storage and are never sent to any server. The user must explicitly click "Export JSON" to share logs with an instructor. A one‑time consent dialog informs the user about local storage usage before any logging occurs. This complies with KVKK Article 4 (lawful processing) and GDPR Article 5 (storage limitation).
proposalState.dataCollectionPlan: Data collected: (1) per‑stage scores (alignment, sizing, spacing, hierarchy, consistency percentages), (2) completion time per stage, (3) number of reset/hint actions, (4) device view switches, (5) final average score across all devices. All data is aggregated locally. For instructor assessment, students export a JSON file containing their anonymised session data (no name, no student ID). The instructor may optionally collect the JSON files via LMS (e.g., Teams, Blackboard). No cloud sync, no third‑party analytics.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Test on Chrome, Firefox, Edge latest versions. Use feature detection for drag/resize. Provide fallback message: “Update your browser for best experience.”
Include persistent help bar with icons, a “Hint” button, and a 30‑second interactive tutorial on first load showing how to drag and resize.
Auto‑save every 30 seconds. Display prominent reminder to export JSON after each session. Provide “Restore from backup” option if corruption detected.
Use compassion model (no point loss). Provide encouraging feedback, reset button, hints, and a “Try Again” option. Show improvement over multiple attempts.

proposalState.facilitatorRole: The facilitator (course instructor or TA) introduces the game in lab session, monitors student progress via exported JSON files, and leads a 10‑minute debrief discussing common UI/UX mistakes found during gameplay.
proposalState.wf_dash: The main game screen shows a top header with game logo, five stage progress indicators (dots), and average score. Below, device tabs (Phone, Tablet, Desktop) let players switch views. A task banner displays the mission, hint chips, and live score. The central device frame contains draggable UI elements. Bottom controls include Reset, Hint, and Submit buttons, plus a help bar.
proposalState.wf_asset: Within each device frame (phone: 390×780, tablet: 768×580, desktop: 860×560), UI elements (buttons, inputs, labels, cards) are absolutely positioned. Each draggable element shows a resize handle at bottom‑right corner. Selected elements have a glowing outline. When dragged, elements move smoothly; when resized, dimensions update in real time.
proposalState.wf_portfolio: After submission, a grade overlay appears with letter grade (A+ to F), score percentage, and a horizontal bar. Below, per‑criterion breakdown (alignment, sizing, spacing, hierarchy, consistency) with individual bars and percentages. Feedback text explains strengths/weaknesses. Buttons: “Try Again” (reset current device) and “Next: (device/stage)”. Final summary shows all stage results.
proposalState.inScope: fixing messy UI elements via drag & drop and resize on phone/tablet/desktop; live scoring based on alignment, sizing, spacing, hierarchy, consistency; 5 progressive stages (login, navigation, cards, payment, settings); grade overlay with A+ to F and per‑criterion breakdown; reset and hint buttons; export of session data as JSON. Compassion model applied: losePoints=0 – no punitive point loss; players never lose points, only gain from improvements.
proposalState.outScope: real‑time multiplayer; leaderboards; online accounts or cloud save; artificial intelligence or machine learning; payment or subscription; mobile app (runs in browser only); animated tutorials beyond help bar; server‑side data collection; third‑party analytics; quizzes or written tests; instructor dashboard (instructors review exported JSON files manually).
proposalState.whyMotivation: Students learn UI/UX principles (alignment, hierarchy, consistency, spacing, touch targets) through hands‑on correction instead of passive lectures. Self-Determination Theory is supported: autonomy (free drag/resize), competence (live score and grade feedback), relatedness (device views simulate real user contexts). Octalysis white‑hat drives (Epic Meaning, Accomplishment, Empowerment) keep engagement high. The compassion model (no point loss) reduces anxiety and encourages experimentation.
proposalState.howEthics: No personal data collected – all logs stay in browser local storage. User must explicitly export JSON to share. A one‑time consent dialog informs about local storage usage (KVKK/GDPR compliant). No punitive scoring (losePoints=0) – players only gain points. Inclusive design: help bar, hint button, resettable stages, no time pressure. HEXAD player types all supported (Achiever, Philanthropist, Free Spirit, Socialiser, Player, Disruptor). No dark patterns or addictive mechanics.

ideaBrief.title: IKU WEBGAMES

ideaBrief.successVision: Transform passive SAD learning into active, discovery-based farm management with casual minigames

proposalState.problemStatement: Students in COM4061 often struggle to actively engage with abstract systems analysis concepts, relying heavily on passive reading. Due to this lack of interactive practice, approximately 65% of students make fundamental errors in use case diagrams, and 50% fail to correctly identify functional requirements. Traditional educational materials fail to provide the motivation and immediate feedback necessary for skill mastery. A gamified web platform featuring a fully customizable farm tycoon, casual learning minigames, XP-based progression, and a rewarding worker-hire system addresses this engagement gap by transforming passive study into an active, discovery-based learning experience.

teams.title: Use Case Quest: From Actor to System Designer

proposalState.projectTitle: Use Case Quest: From Actor to System Designer
proposalState.organisation: Istanbul Kultur University, Department of Computer Engineering
proposalState.domain: Educational Technology / Gamified Learning / Software Analysis Education
proposalState.deliverableArea: Browser-based educational game for teaching Use Case Diagrams
proposalState.problemStatement: In COM4061 System Analysis and Design, beginner learners often memorize use case terms without being able to apply them correctly. In a target cohort of approximately 120 first-time software engineering students, at least 40% are expected to confuse actor vs non-actor elements and to misplace use cases inside or outside the system boundary during early UML practice. The project addresses this measurable competency gap by building a replayable browser-based learning game that teaches, reinforces, and assesses use case concepts through guided practice and increasing difficulty.
proposalState.targetPopulation: The target population is approximately 120 undergraduate COM4061 students and other beginner UML learners who are studying Use Case Diagrams for the first time.
successCriteria: Achieve at least 80% correct actor vs non-actor classification after Level 1.
Achieve at least 75% correct system-boundary placement accuracy after the boundary level.

At least 70% of players complete all core use case learning levels in one session.

At least 75% of players score 70 XP or more in the final use case assessment sequence.
At least 80% of students retain core use case concepts in a 48-hour delayed quiz.
Reduce actor/use case placement errors by at least 40% compared to baseline practice results.

proposalState.narrativeTheme: Use Case Quest: From Actor to System Designer is set in a digital training academy where beginner UML learners progress through a sequence of interactive rooms designed to teach Use Case Diagrams step by step. In each room, the learner faces a different challenge related to core use case concepts, including identifying actors, distinguishing non-actors, recognizing valid use cases, placing elements correctly with respect to the system boundary, and constructing accurate actor–use case relationships. The game is grounded in Self-Determination Theory by supporting autonomy through difficulty selection and replayability, competence through immediate feedback and progressive challenge, and relatedness through rescue progression and shared competition. It also follows the Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics approach by combining interactive mechanics, repeated learning dynamics, and a strong sense of mastery and advancement. The instructional progression is aligned with Bloom Taxonomy, moving learners from remembering and understanding definitions to applying, analysing, evaluating, and finally creating correct use case structures. The design also incorporates HEXAD player types so that different learner motivations, such as achievement, exploration, social interaction, contribution, and competition, are addressed within the same system. In addition, the project is supported by Experiential Learning Theory, because learners do not only study definitions but actively learn through doing, testing, reflecting, correcting, and replaying. Finally, the overall effectiveness of the project is evaluated through Kirkpatrick Four-Level Evaluation, so that reaction, learning, behaviour change, and educational results can all be assessed in a structured way.
proposalState.gameOverview: This application is a replayable educational browser game that teaches Use Case Diagram concepts through guided interaction and increasing difficulty. The player chooses a difficulty mode, enters the academy, and completes learning rooms that explain and reinforce what an actor is, what a non-actor is, what a use case is, how the system boundary works, and how actors relate to use cases. Instead of learning only through static notes, the player actively classifies, places, connects, and evaluates diagram elements. Each completed room provides explanatory feedback, XP, progression, and motivation for replay. In this way, the game turns abstract use case theory into practical and memorable learning.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: The application supports autonomy by allowing the learner to choose easy, medium, or hard mode, replay levels, improve previous scores, and progress at their own pace while exploring different use case learning challenges.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: The application supports competence by giving clear goals, immediate feedback, progressive difficulty, repeated practice, and visible improvement through XP, completed levels, and better performance in use case modeling tasks.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: The application supports relatedness by connecting learning to a shared rescue mission, showing classmates being saved, and using leaderboard competition so players feel part of a wider learning community.

conceptMappings.mechanic: The player separates actor and non-actor items in the first challenge room by dragging them into correct categories.
The player matches system goals and user actions to valid use cases, learning what the system actually does.
The player places actions and entities inside or outside the system boundary to understand what belongs to the system and what does not.
The player connects actors to the correct use cases with line-drawing interactions in association puzzles.
The player orders interaction steps in the correct sequence to build a logical use case scenario.
Easy, medium, and hard modes adjust complexity so learners can revisit the same concepts at different challenge levels.
XP, rescued learners, replayability, and leaderboard ranking encourage repeated play and deeper learning.

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: The application uses level progression, interactive diagram construction, drag-and-drop classification, scenario-based tasks, difficulty modes, feedback loops, rescue progression, unlockable content, leaderboard competition, and XP accumulation to teach use case modeling actively.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: Players repeatedly learn, test, fail safely, retry, and improve their understanding of actors, non-actors, use cases, and boundaries through guided interaction. Increasing difficulty, replayability, and progression create sustained motivation and repeated practice.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: The player experiences curiosity, mastery, urgency, progression, and achievement while learning UML concepts. The game creates a feeling of growth, challenge, and escape, while also supporting replay, competition, and structured educational progress.

scenarios.title: Actor and Non-Actor Classification
scenarios.setup: The player enters the first training room of the Use Case Academy and sees a mixed set of people, systems, devices, and objects related to a school management system.
scenarios.winCondition: The player correctly classifies all given items as actor or non-actor and explains the difference through the puzzle interaction with at least 80% accuracy.
scenarios.loseCondition: If the player makes too many incorrect placements, the round ends with guidance feedback only; losePoints=0 and no punitive penalty is applied.
scenarios.outcome: The player learns the meaning of actor, non-actor, and external system participation, gains XP, and unlocks the next concept room.
scenarios.neutralCondition: If the player finishes with partial correctness, the system gives corrective hints and allows replay without score loss.

scenarios.title: System Boundary and Use Case Placement
scenarios.setup: The player enters a boundary-design room where actions, users, and system functions must be placed correctly inside or outside the system boundary.
scenarios.winCondition: The player correctly places all use cases and external entities in relation to the boundary and completes the task within the expected logic rules.
scenarios.loseCondition: If the player submits incorrect placements, the system provides explanatory feedback, keeps progress supportive, and uses no punitive scoring; losePoints=0.
scenarios.outcome: The player understands what belongs to the system, what stays outside it, and how use cases are defined as system services.
scenarios.neutralCondition: If the player gets some items correct and some wrong, the correct ones stay highlighted and the player retries only the incorrect items.

scenarios.title: Actor–Use Case Relationship Construction
scenarios.setup: The player reaches an advanced room where different actors must be linked to the correct use cases based on their interactions with the system.
scenarios.winCondition: The player creates all correct actor–use case connections and demonstrates the relationship structure without invalid links.
scenarios.loseCondition: Wrong links do not create harsh failure; the system removes only invalid lines, gives guidance, and keeps the compassion model active with no punitive penalty.
scenarios.outcome: The player learns how actors interact with use cases, strengthens modeling accuracy, and earns XP for progression and leaderboard ranking.
scenarios.neutralCondition: If the player forms a partially correct network, the system marks correct associations and invites revision of the remaining links.

frs.description: The system shall provide a guided learning flow that teaches the definitions of actor, non-actor, and use case before assessment activities begin.
The system shall allow the learner to classify items as actor, non-actor, or use case through interactive drag-and-drop and selection-based exercises.
The system shall provide system boundary activities in which learners place actors outside the boundary and use cases inside the boundary.
The system shall support three game modes: Easy, Medium, and Hard, with increasing complexity, reduced hints, and more challenging content.
The system shall assign score and XP after each completed level and calculate a cumulative total score and total XP for the player.
The system shall store player profiles, completed levels, total XP, total score, and leaderboard ranking in a database.

The system shall display a leaderboard that compares players based on score, XP, and completion performance.

proposalState.inScope: This project includes a browser-based educational game that teaches use case diagram concepts through interactive play. The system covers actor, non-actor, use case, system boundary, actor–use case relationships, include, extend, and related UML learning tasks. It includes easy, medium, and hard difficulty modes, replayability, XP-based progression, leaderboard support, notebook summaries, and a compassion model with no punitive penalty and losePoints=0.
proposalState.outScope: This project does not include full coverage of all UML diagram types beyond the selected learning path, teacher-side grading dashboards, advanced analytics panels, multiplayer live chat, or a complete enterprise learning management system integration. High-end custom art, voice acting, and mobile-native deployment are also outside the current scope.
proposalState.whyMotivation: The motivation of this project is to make use case diagram learning more active, memorable, and engaging. Many students find UML too abstract when it is taught only with slides and definitions. This game transforms theory into practice by letting players learn concepts step by step, apply them immediately, replay levels at different difficulty modes, and stay motivated through progression, rescue narrative, XP, and leaderboard competition.
proposalState.howEthics: The project is designed with an ethical and learner-centered approach. It avoids punitive failure systems and instead supports retry, feedback, and improvement through a compassion model. Personal data collection is minimized, leaderboard data should be limited to necessary player identifiers, and privacy principles such as consent, data protection, and KVKK/GDPR awareness should be respected. The system is intended to support learning, not to pressure or shame players.

proposalState.problemStatement: Students in System Analysis and Design consistently struggle to internalize which attributes and methods belong to which UML class. In a preliminary informal assessment of COM4061 students, fewer than 40% could correctly assign all members to a given class diagram from memory without visual reference. Traditional instruction through static slides and textbook diagrams leads to passive recognition rather than active recall. Students can identify class diagram components in isolation but fail to correctly associate members to their parent class under timed conditions. Existing digital tools present UML as drag-and-drop construction exercises, which test assembly skill rather than membership recognition, leaving a gap in rapid identification practice.
proposalState.targetPopulation: Undergraduate students enrolled in System Analysis and Design (COM4061) at Istanbul Kultur University, Year 3/4, approximately 60 students per cohort
successCriteria: All 8 levels are completable from start to finish without crashes or game-breaking bugs (Kirkpatrick L1)
Post-play quiz scores show >=20% improvement over pre-play baseline on UML class membership questions (Kirkpatrick L2)
>=70% of players correctly identify class members they previously misattributed after playing (Kirkpatrick L3)
Players rate the experience >=3.8/5
Level 8 completion rate used as mastery proxy, target >=50% of players reach and complete Level 8

proposalState.narrativeTheme: UML Defender applies Self-Determination Theory through player-chosen upgrades and difficulty presets, granting autonomy over playstyle. The MDA Framework structures the shoot-or-pass mechanic as a learning dynamic where repeated color-coded feedback builds class membership recognition. Bloom Taxonomy organises the 8-level arc from Remember through Create, scaffolded by a fading reference panel. Flow Theory is maintained through escalating fall speed and spawn rate that match growing player competence. Bartle Types and the HEXAD model inform the Achiever-weighted design with progression unlocks and score tracking that reward mastery-oriented play.
proposalState.gameOverview: UML Defender is a browser-based 2D educational arcade shooter built in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript where the player controls a UML class node at the bottom of the screen. Members fall from above, color-coded blue for own class and red for imposters. The player must shoot red imposters and let blue members dock safely. Each of the 8 levels represents a distinct UML class (Animal, BankAccount, Student, Vehicle, Book, Employee, Dog, Hospital) with accurate attributes and methods drawn from COM4061 course material. A class reference panel on the left shows the full class definition in early levels and progressively fades, removing attributes, then methods, then the panel entirely by Level 8. Between levels the player selects upgrades from categories including weapons, speed, shields, and abilities such as X-Ray Scan which temporarily reveals all member identities. Four difficulty presets (Easy, Normal, Hard, Insane) control fall speed, spawn rate, and starting lives. The game runs entirely client-side with zero network calls.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Players choose which difficulty preset to play on (Easy, Normal, Hard, Insane) and select their own upgrade path between levels from a randomized set of enhancements. No two playthroughs follow the same upgrade trajectory. The game never forces a single strategy - players can prioritize speed, firepower, defense, or abilities based on their own assessment of what they need. The X-Ray Scan and EMP are activated by player choice, not triggered automatically.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Each correct kill or successful dock gives immediate visual and score feedback confirming the player's knowledge. The fading reference panel creates a visible competence arc - early levels support the player with full class definitions, and later levels remove that support, making the player aware of their own growing mastery. Level completion is gated by kill count, so advancement is always earned through demonstrated recognition skill. Upgrades between levels reward accumulated competence with tangible power increases.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: The post-session classroom debrief creates shared experience among students - comparing which levels were hardest, which classes were most confusing, and which upgrade strategies worked best. The game's arcade format and score display create natural conversation starters without requiring in-game social features. The shared struggle of Level 8 with no reference panel produces a common challenge experience across the cohort.

rewardStructure.points: Score awarded per correct imposter kill (+100 base + level scaling), member dock (+60), and deducted for friendly fire (-50). Accuracy percentage tracked and displayed.
rewardStructure.badges: No explicit badge system. Level completion and final victory screen serve as achievement markers.
rewardStructure.progression: 8-level arc with escalating difficulty. Upgrade selection between levels provides permanent power growth. Reference panel fade creates a visible competence progression.
rewardStructure.elite: Victory screen with "UML MASTER" title, final score, accuracy percentage, and total kills displayed. Awarded for completing all 8 levels on Normal difficulty or above with ≥80% accuracy. The player has demonstrated full mastery of class membership across all 8 UML classes without reference support.
rewardStructure.professional: Level completion banner with score breakdown and upgrade summary. Awarded for completing each level by reaching the required kill count. Each level cleared adds a permanent upgrade to the player's arsenal, providing tangible power growth that reflects accumulated knowledge.
rewardStructure.access: Upgrade selection screen between levels offering 3-6 cards from weapon, speed, shield, and ability categories. Awarded simply for completing any level regardless of score or accuracy. Every player who advances gets to choose an upgrade, ensuring forward momentum and a sense of progression even with imperfect performance.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Blue member tokens labeled with attribute notation (e.g. '- balance: double')
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player reads the label and lets blue tokens dock to score points
conceptMappings.rationale: Forces active reading of attribute names under time pressure rather than passive recognition

conceptMappings.mechanic: Blue member tokens labeled with method notation (e.g. '+ withdraw(): bool')
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player distinguishes methods from attributes and associates both with the current class
conceptMappings.rationale: Builds recognition that classes contain both data and behavior

conceptMappings.mechanic: Red imposter tokens containing members from other classes
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player must shoot tokens that do not belong to the current class
conceptMappings.rationale: Forces discrimination between similar-looking members across different classes

conceptMappings.mechanic: Fading reference panel showing the full UML class definition
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player transitions from assisted lookup to unassisted recall across 8 levels
conceptMappings.rationale: Scaffolds learning by gradually removing support as competence grows

conceptMappings.mechanic: Imposter pool drawn from 7 other classes with plausible-sounding members
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player encounters members that could belong to multiple classes and must decide quickly
conceptMappings.rationale: Targets the specific confusion students face when classes have overlapping terminology

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Shoot-or-let-pass decision on each color-coded falling member, fading class reference panel that removes assistance over 8 levels, upgrade selection screen between levels offering weapon and ability enhancements, 4 difficulty presets affecting speed and spawn rate, kill-count-based level progression, score and accuracy tracking displayed in real-time HUD, X-Ray Scan ability revealing identities temporarily once per level.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: The red/blue color coding creates an immediate feedback loop where players associate specific labels with class membership through repetition. The fading reference panel shifts gameplay from assisted lookup to unassisted recall across the 8-level arc. Upgrade choices between levels create a self-directed difficulty curve where players prioritize offensive or defensive strategies. Increasing fall speed and spawn rates force progressively faster recognition, building automaticity in class member identification.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: Challenge (increasing speed and disappearing reference panel demand faster recall), Discovery (encountering 8 new UML classes across the game), Sensation (particle effects, screen flash, and score popups on correct kills and docks), Submission (arcade flow state under sustained time pressure), Expression (upgrade path choices let players shape their own playstyle).

proposalState.whyMotivation: UML class membership is a foundational skill in System Analysis and Design that students consistently underperform on in assessments. Traditional methods do not build the rapid, automatic recognition needed. An arcade shooter format delivers high-repetition exposure in an engaging format that transforms passive study into active discrimination practice. The no-punitive scoring model ensures wrong identifications cost points but never block progression, keeping motivation intact.
proposalState.howEthics: No personal data is collected, no login is required, and zero network calls are made. Shooting a blue own member results in score deduction only with no punitive life loss, normalising mistakes as part of the learning process rather than penalising them harshly. Participation does not affect the course grade in any way. All session data is local to the browser and never visible to the instructor during play. The game presents no violent, discriminatory, or culturally insensitive content.

teams.title: SAD Highway — A Reflex-Paced Driving Game for Reading UML Domain-Model Class Dia

ideaBrief.title: SAD Highway
ideaBrief.domain: Higher-ed software engineering education (System Analysis & Design)
ideaBrief.audience: ≈80 third-year IT undergraduates at Istanbul Kultur University taking COM4061 in Fall semester
ideaBrief.coreMetaphor: A single car races down a multi-lane highway whose road signs cycle through the four core dialects of System Analysis & Design: Use Case vocabulary (ACTOR, TRIGGER, SCENARIO), User Story vocabulary (AS A, I WANT, SO THAT, GIVEN, WHEN, THEN), Requirement vocabulary (FUNCTIONAL, NON-FUNCTIONAL, MOSCOW, MUST, SHOULD) and Domain Modelling vocabulary (CLASS, ATTRIBUTE, ASSOCIATION, MULTIPLICITY, AGGREGATION). The driver dodges obstacle-cones (red-herring wrong-keyword distractors) by steering left/right between lanes, and when a fill-in-the-blank question appears the car automatically slows down so the student can read the prompt and steer into the lane carrying the correct missing letter to complete the English term.
ideaBrief.successVision: After three 20-minute sessions a COM4061 student can correctly fill ≥80% of mixed fill-in-the-blank SAD questions under time pressure across all four topic clusters (Use Cases, User Stories, Requirements, Domain Modelling), outperform a lecture-only control group by ≥30% on the standard COM4061 mid-term, and report ≥4.0/5 enjoyment.

proposalState.problemStatement: Internal COM4061 analytics at Istanbul Kultur University over the last 3 terms show that 62% of third-year IT students confuse use-case structural elements (Actor vs Scenario, Trigger vs Precondition), 58% cannot reliably distinguish Functional from Non-Functional requirements on the mid-term, and 49% mislabel multiplicity or association in their first domain-model assignment. Only 28% can complete a mixed-topic SAD vocabulary template under exam time pressure even after full lecture coverage, leaving downstream design and capstone work weakly grounded.
proposalState.targetPopulation: ≈80 third-year IT undergraduates (ages 20-23) at Istanbul Kultur University enrolled in COM4061 across one Fall cohort, plus a smaller Spring resit cohort of ≈25 students used as a delayed-retest sample.
successCriteria: Increase post-test mixed-vocabulary accuracy by ≥30% over the lecture-only baseline cohort across all four topic clusters
Reduce confusion error rate between Actor / Scenario / Trigger from 62% to <20% in the post quiz
Reduce Functional vs Non-Functional Requirement misclassification from 58% to <25%
Reduce Association / Multiplicity domain-model errors from 49% to <20%
Achieve ≥80% of students completing at least 3 full game sessions (≈20 minutes each) inside the 2-week intervention window
≥75% retention of the core vocabulary in a 48-hour delayed quiz, with mean enjoyment ≥4.0/5

proposalState.narrativeTheme: SAD Highway is a single-lane-discipline driving game set on a stylised three-lane road that stretches across the curriculum of COM4061 System Analysis and Design at Istanbul Kultur University. The student plays a junior analyst commuting between project sites in a small city, and every commute is a chance to sharpen the four dialects of the course at the same time: the use-case dialect (Actor, Trigger, Scenario, Precondition, Postcondition, Main Flow, Alternate Flow), the user-story dialect (As a, I want, So that, Given, When, Then), the requirements dialect (Functional, Non-Functional, MoSCoW, Must, Should), and the domain-modelling dialect (Class, Attribute, Association, Multiplicity, Aggregation). The car rolls forward under its own power; the player steers only left and right, dodging cones placed by careless contractors of misconceptions. Periodically a billboard rises out of the asphalt with a fill-in-the-blank English term and the car automatically eases off the accelerator so the analyst can read it, drawing on the autonomy, competence, and relatedness pillars of Self-Determination Theory to give the player a moment of unhurried choice. The fill-in-the-blank gates are designed through the Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics lens of Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics so that the mechanic of single-letter lane selection produces the dynamic of micro-deliberation under mild speed and the aesthetic of focused calm, and cluster context-switching from one gate to the next forces flexible recall instead of narrow memorisation. Each question is tagged across the six cognitive ranks of Bloom Taxonomy from L1 vocabulary recall up to L4 scenario analysis, and the instructor dashboard projects learning evidence onto all four tiers of Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation — Reaction at session end, Learning through pre/post quizzes, Behaviour in downstream COM4061 assignments, and Results in cohort capstone outcomes. The player archetypes targeted are balanced through the HEXAD player archetypes lens — Achievers chase per-cluster mastery bars, Players chase streak scores, Free Spirits pick which cluster to focus a run on, Philanthropists help classmates in the in-class debrief, Socialisers compare anonymous cluster leaderboards, and the small Disruptor share is served by an optional speed-run mode. Failure is treated with compassion: losePoints=0, no death screen, no punitive penalty; every missed question becomes a Review Mode card with a friendly one-line English explanation, so the highway is always a place to learn faster, never a place to be punished.
proposalState.gameOverview: SAD Highway is played in 15–25-minute single-player sessions in a web browser. The player drives a small top-down car forward on a three-lane highway, steering left or right with arrow keys, A/D, or touch. Procedural cones must be dodged, and every 25–35 seconds a fill-in-the-blank checkpoint appears from one of four English-vocabulary clusters — Use Cases, User Stories, Requirements (FR/NFR/MoSCoW), or Domain Modelling. The car automatically slows down, the prompt is read, then three answer lanes appear each labelled with one candidate letter. The lane the player is in when they cross the answer line is recorded as their answer; immediate feedback shows the full English term and a short explanation. Streaks raise difficulty; misses lower it; at the end of a run the player sees a per-cluster accuracy bar and can replay missed prompts in calm Review Mode, optionally filtered by cluster. The whole experience is woven from the Self-Determination Theory and Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics design lenses, mapped to Bloom Taxonomy levels, balanced across HEXAD player archetypes, and assessed through Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Players choose their driving pace, which lane to favour, whether to prefer a balanced cluster mix or focus a run on their weakest cluster, when to push forward aggressively and when to coast, and whether to play a fresh run or jump straight into Review Mode of past misses; settings let them turn on a dyslexia-friendly font and pick a lane-colour palette — autonomy is preserved across input, pacing, content focus, and review at all times.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Repeated short rounds with immediate per-question feedback let competence grow visibly session over session; the end-of-run per-cluster bars and the rising adaptive difficulty give clear signals of mastery across Use Cases, User Stories, Requirements, and Domain Modelling; correctly filling a streak of mixed-cluster terms produces visible XP and unlocks slightly harder question clusters with longer terms.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: Anonymous aggregate class-level leaderboards by cluster accuracy let students see they are part of a learning cohort without naming-and-shaming; a shared in-class debrief screen lets the instructor walk through the prompts the whole cohort missed most, creating a sense of shared journey through the full COM4061 syllabus rather than a private struggle with vocabulary.

rewardStructure.elite: Top 5% by post-test improvement across all four clusters — SAD Highway Master badge displayed on the COM4061 cohort honour wall, recommended for the spring teaching-assistant pool, and invitation to co-author the next semester's question bank.
rewardStructure.professional: Top 25% — SAD Analyst badge, signed instructor recommendation letter for internship applications, and early access to the COM4061 design-phase follow-up game.
rewardStructure.access: All participants — Highway Driver badge, downloadable PDF cheat-sheet covering every cluster encountered, peer-reviewed reflection portfolio entry, and unlimited Review Mode access.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Use-case-cluster billboard prompt where the missing letter completes ACT_R / TRIGG_R style English term
Green-tinted gate (before) vs red-tinted gate (after) framed as 'true before driving through' vs 'true after driving through'
Centre lane vs side lanes — choosing a side lane during a narrative segment models taking an alternate flow

conceptMappings.concept: User Story template (As a / I want / So that)
conceptMappings.mechanic: Three consecutive user-story-cluster gates within one segment, each filling one slot of the user-story sentence
End-of-segment three-card mini-quiz where the student steers to the lane labelled Given, When, or Then
Requirements-cluster prompt where the player steers to the lane labelled FR or NFR for a given requirement sentence
Four-letter fill prompt where the player completes M_ST, SHO_LD, etc. with single-letter lane choice
Domain-cluster prompt where the missing letter completes CLA_S or ATT_IBUTE
Two-gate combo: first complete the term ASSOC_ATION, then a 1..*, *..1, 0..1 multiplicity choice via three lanes

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Lane-based steering (3 lanes, left/right discrete moves with 250 ms transition), procedural obstacle-cone spawning, time-windowed question gates with automatic deceleration, single-correct-letter answer lanes, immediate feedback, cluster-mixing per run, score and streak, end-of-run per-cluster accuracy breakdown, Review Mode filterable by cluster, instructor CSV/JSON export of anonymised audit log.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: A rhythm emerges where the student alternates between fast reflex driving and slower deliberate reading; cluster context-switching forces flexible recall instead of rote memorisation; difficulty rises with sustained accuracy and falls after misses, producing a self-balancing flow curve; streaks create rising tension while the auto-slow at each gate gives a guaranteed cognitive breath; post-run Review converts losses into learning across the weakest cluster first.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: Felt experience: focused alertness during driving, micro-relief and curiosity at each gate, small triumphs on correct fills, gentle compassion on misses (no harsh penalty, no death screen, losePoints=0 — no punitive mechanic), and a sense of broad mastery on the end-of-run cluster bars.

scenarios.title: First-Drive Mixed Vocabulary Run
scenarios.setup: A new player starts a fresh session; the question bank is restricted to L1 (Remember) vocabulary terms but draws from all four clusters so the player sees the breadth of COM4061 immediately.
scenarios.winCondition: Player completes ≥6 question gates with ≥80% correct in a single 15-minute run, with at least one gate from each of the four clusters
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 — no punitive scoring; if the player crashes or answers wrong, the car simply respawns one lane behind and the missed term goes into Review Mode (compassion model, no punitive penalty)
scenarios.outcome: Player unlocks the L2–L3 difficulty tier and sees a per-cluster accuracy bar that points to the cluster they should focus on next
scenarios.neutralCondition: If the player exits mid-run the partial event log is preserved for resume

scenarios.title: Cross-Cluster Confusion Round
scenarios.setup: After two clean runs, the system spawns a difficulty spike that specifically alternates clusters every gate (Use Case → Requirement → Domain → User Story → repeat) to force flexible recall and surface confusions between similar-sounding terms (Scenario vs Story, FR vs Use Case, Association vs Aggregation).
scenarios.winCondition: Player correctly answers ≥4 of 5 mixed-cluster prompts in the spike segment
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 — no penalty; every missed item is added to Review Mode with a side-by-side comparison card across the two clusters that were confused (no punitive, compassion model)
scenarios.outcome: Cross-cluster confusion rate drops in the next run; the misclassification cluster pair is logged for the instructor dashboard
scenarios.neutralCondition: Player may toggle to Review Mode at any time without penalty

scenarios.title: Domain-Model Speed Lane
scenarios.setup: Optional speed-run mode targeting Disruptor and Player HEXAD archetypes; three back-to-back gates form a small domain-model fragment: complete CLASS → ATTRIBUTE → MULTIPLICITY in one timed segment.
scenarios.winCondition: Player fills all three domain-model slots correctly inside the timed segment
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 — the segment ends gracefully, the partial fragment is shown in Review Mode for compassionate retry (no punitive)
scenarios.outcome: Player earns a Domain Sprinter badge and unlocks a follow-up Acceptance-Criteria (Given/When/Then) mini-round
scenarios.neutralCondition: Player may skip the speed lane entirely and stay on the normal track

userStories.soThat: I demonstrate I know the SAD term being asked
userStories.acc: Given three lettered lanes appear, When the car passes the answer line, Then the lane the car is in is recorded as my answer and immediate feedback is shown

frs.description: The system shall render a top-down 3-lane highway at ≥55 fps on Chrome/Edge/Safari current-version desktops and recent iPad/Android tablets.
The system shall accept ArrowLeft/ArrowRight, A/D, and left/right touch swipe as steering inputs, with input-to-render latency ≤50 ms.
The system shall spawn obstacle cones procedurally with difficulty scaling tied to elapsed time and recent accuracy.
The system shall trigger a fill-in-the-blank question gate every 25–35 seconds of driving, automatically reducing car speed to 35% during the gate.

The system shall draw a fill-in-the-blank English prompt from a curated, versioned multi-cluster question bank of ≥80 items grouped into four clusters — Use Cases, User Stories, Requirements (FR/NFR/MoSCoW), and Domain Modelling.
The system shall mix clusters within a single run so that at least 3 of the 4 clusters appear in any session of ≥10 gates.
The system shall present three lane choices each carrying a single candidate letter, with exactly one correct letter for the target term.

The system shall log every player action, question gate, answer event, and difficulty change as an audit-grade event record stored in a session log so instructors can audit a recording of each playthrough.
The system shall expose an anonymised CSV/JSON export of the audit event log to the instructor role.
The system shall provide a post-run Review Mode replaying only missed questions with the full term, a ≤120-char English explanation, and a worked cluster example.
The system shall let the student filter Review Mode by cluster (Use Cases / User Stories / Requirements / Domain Modelling).

nfrs.desc: Stable steering loop on a mid-range laptop and iPad-class tablet under normal driving + question rendering
nfrs.target: ≥55 fps p95, input latency ≤50 ms

nfrs.desc: GDPR and KVKK compliance — no personally identifiable information beyond a randomised session id; consent obtained at first launch; aggregate telemetry only
nfrs.target: Zero PII stored beyond debrief; consent log retained

nfrs.desc: WCAG 2.1 AA contrast for prompts, colour-blind safe lane palette, dyslexia-friendly font option, full keyboard control
nfrs.target: Contrast ratio ≥4.5:1, all interactions reachable without mouse

nfrs.desc: First-time onboarding to confident driving
nfrs.target: <3 minutes from first launch to first successful question-gate completion

nfrs.desc: Question bank loaded from a versioned JSON file separable from game code, organised by cluster
nfrs.target: Adding a new question requires only JSON edit and CI re-run, no code change

nfrs.desc: Browser refresh tolerance during a session
nfrs.target: Mid-session state recoverable within ≤2 s with no data loss on event log

nfrs.desc: GDPR + KVKK compliance — explicit informed consent screen at first launch; only random session id and anonymised event log stored
nfrs.target: No PII in any exported file

proposalState.logPrivacy: Every event in the audit log records a randomised session id only; no names, no emails, no IP addresses, no device fingerprints. The log can be flushed by the student at any time from the settings screen, and only aggregate per-question statistics are exposed to the instructor. The log is retained in browser local storage by default and uploaded only with explicit student consent, in line with GDPR Article 6(1)(a) and KVKK Article 5/1.
proposalState.dataCollectionPlan: Each playthrough produces an event log of question_id, cluster, category, chosen_letter, correct, response_ms, gate_speed, and session_id. Logs are stored locally and aggregated per-question for the instructor dashboard. No video, no audio, no PII. Students review and approve before any upload. Data retention is 12 weeks, after which raw logs are deleted and only category-level aggregates remain.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Frame-rate and input-latency budget enforced from sprint 1 with automated perf tests on every PR; reduce visual effects on low-end devices via a quality-auto setting
tuslerRisks.preventive: Performance budget defined in NFR-01 and tracked in CI
tuslerRisks.responsive: Auto-downscale particles and shadows if fps drops below 45

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Privacy-by-design review of every event-log field with the supervisor before sprint 2; KVKK/GDPR consent screen and zero-PII contract enforced in code review
tuslerRisks.preventive: Privacy checklist in the PR template
tuslerRisks.responsive: If a PII field slips in, rotate session ids and purge affected logs within 24 h

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Iterate steering feel weekly with 5-student playtest sessions; tune lane-shift time, deceleration curve, and gate spacing each sprint
tuslerRisks.preventive: Weekly hallway playtests built into sprint plan
tuslerRisks.responsive: Roll back to previous tuning parameters via versioned config

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Maintain a small backup question bank per cluster and accept minor typo fixes via JSON-only PRs to keep churn low
tuslerRisks.preventive: Lint check on the cluster JSON files
tuslerRisks.responsive: Hot-fix typos as JSON-only deploys

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Run a small pilot with 8 students before the full cohort launch to catch question-bank ambiguity, cluster-mixing pacing, instruction wording, and accessibility issues
tuslerRisks.preventive: Pilot scheduled in the project plan
tuslerRisks.responsive: Adjust prompt wording or remove ambiguous items based on pilot data

proposalState.facilitatorRole: The classroom facilitator runs a 5-minute pre-session briefing on the rules, the four clusters, and the consent screen, monitors the room during driving sessions to catch accessibility issues, leads a 10-minute post-session debrief projecting the most-missed prompts per cluster to the whole cohort, and collects the anonymised CSV export at the end of the week to update lecture emphasis for the next class.
proposalState.wf_dash: Top bar shows session id, elapsed time, current streak, and a small per-cluster mini-bar of running accuracy (UC / US / REQ / DM). Centre fills the 3-lane top-down highway with the car at the lower centre, cones and gates approaching from the top, and an upcoming-question banner one-third up from the bottom carrying a small cluster tag. Right rail (collapsible on tablet) lists the last 3 answered prompts with a green/red tick and cluster tag. Bottom: pause button, settings (font, palette, cluster focus), and an always-visible 'Open Review Mode' link. All interactive targets are at least 44 px to fit thumb-reach on tablet.
proposalState.wf_asset: The Question-Gate asset is a horizontal billboard rendered just above the answer line. Top: cluster tag chip (USE CASE / USER STORY / REQUIREMENT / DOMAIN) in high-contrast colour. Centre: the fill-in-the-blank English prompt in a dyslexia-friendly font at 32 px (e.g. 'A person or system that interacts with the system is called an A_TOR'). Below the prompt: three lane-letter tiles aligned to the three lanes, each 96 px wide, each carrying one candidate letter. A 1.5 s countdown ring shows time remaining until the car crosses the answer line.
proposalState.wf_portfolio: Per-student portfolio surface: title bar with a randomised session id (no name shown by default), three tabs — Runs (chronological list of sessions with score, accuracy, duration, cluster breakdown), Misses (every prompt the student got wrong with the full term and a one-line English explanation, filterable by cluster), and Mastery (a per-cluster radar chart across Use Cases, User Stories, Requirements, and Domain Modelling, plus sub-categories within each). An 'Export to PDF' button at the bottom, a 'Delete my data' button next to it.
proposalState.inScope: In scope: a single-player browser-based driving game with a curated English question bank of ≥80 fill-in-the-blank items across four clusters — Use Cases (Actor / Trigger / Pre-Post / Flows), User Stories (As-a / I-want / So-that / Given-When-Then), Requirements (FR / NFR / MoSCoW), and Domain Modelling (Class / Attribute / Association / Multiplicity / Aggregation); auto-decelerating question gates; cluster-mixing per run; instant feedback; Review Mode of missed items filterable by cluster; anonymised CSV/JSON instructor export; KVKK/GDPR-compliant consent flow. Compassion model: losePoints=0, no punitive scoring, no death screen — every miss is a learning card. Failure is treated as feedback, not penalty, in line with a compassion model of educational game design.
proposalState.outScope: Out of scope for this proposal: multiplayer or networked play, voice input, real-money in-app purchases, 3D graphics, cross-device cloud save, mobile-phone (small-screen) layouts, languages other than English, hardware steering-wheel support, and COM4061 topics outside the four chosen clusters (e.g. detailed deployment diagrams, full project-management content, database physical design).
proposalState.whyMotivation: We are building SAD Highway because three terms of COM4061 quiz data show that lecture-only delivery leaves 62% of third-year IT students confused about basic use-case structure, 58% misclassifying Functional vs Non-Functional requirements, and 49% mislabelling association/multiplicity on their first domain model. A reflex-paced driving frame that mixes all four clusters in one run turns dry vocabulary drills into a flow-state activity that respects student autonomy (their pace, their cluster focus, their settings), grows visible competence (per-cluster mastery bars), and creates relatedness through shared in-class debriefs of the most-missed items — the three pillars of Self-Determination Theory applied to a problem we can actually measure across the whole COM4061 vocabulary, not just one corner of it.
proposalState.howEthics: Ethics: explicit informed consent before any data is recorded, with a simple plain-English KVKK/GDPR consent screen at first launch; zero PII collected (randomised session id only); students can delete their data at any time from the portfolio screen; instructor only sees aggregate per-question statistics, never individual rankings or names; compassion model in the game itself — losePoints=0, no punitive scoring, no public shaming, no death screen, only Review Mode for missed items; accessibility-first design (WCAG 2.1 AA contrast, colour-blind safe palette, dyslexia-friendly font option, full keyboard control) to avoid excluding students with sensory or motor differences.

ideaBrief.title: Runtime Analyst
ideaBrief.domain: Systems Analysis and Design — UML Use Case modelling and requirements engineering
ideaBrief.audience: COM4061 students at Istanbul Kultur University, Department of Computer Engineering
ideaBrief.coreMetaphor: A synthwave arcade runner in which the player is a freelance systems analyst sprinting through a neon city, selecting correct UML concepts at quiz gates and assembling requirement sentences from word cubes collected on the track.
ideaBrief.successVision: After one 15-minute session a student can name, define, and correctly apply all six core SAD concepts — actor, use case, user story, include, extend, and system boundary — and leaves with a personalised Vocabulary panel as a permanent revision reference.

proposalState.organisation: Istanbul Kultur University, Department of Computer Engineering
proposalState.domain: Systems Analysis and Design — UML Use Case modelling and requirements engineering
proposalState.deliverableArea: Single-file browser-based educational endless-runner game (HTML5 Canvas + Vanilla JS, desktop and mobile, no installation required)
proposalState.problemStatement: An informal diagnostic administered to 89 COM4061 Systems Analysis and Design students at Istanbul Kultur University across two consecutive terms revealed that 71% scored below the passing threshold on UML use-case modelling tasks; only 26% could correctly distinguish include from extend relationships when presented with written scenarios, and fewer than 30% produced a syntactically valid Connextra-format user story on the first attempt. Structured observation of weekly lab sessions showed that students who had attended all lectures still averaged 2.4 conceptual errors per use-case diagram. These gaps persisted across three academic terms despite 60-minute lecture interventions, indicating that passive instruction alone is insufficient to build transferable UML modelling competency in this population.
proposalState.targetPopulation: Approximately 90 junior and senior IT and Computer Engineering undergraduates enrolled in COM4061 Systems Analysis and Design at Istanbul Kultur University (Spring 2026 cohort), aged 20-24, who have completed prerequisite programming and data-structures courses and hold surface familiarity with UML notation but demonstrate mid-to-low performance on use-case modelling assessments.
successCriteria: Increase post-test UML use-case modelling accuracy by >=30% over pre-test baseline (measured with same 18-item quiz)
Reduce mean error rate on include/extend distinction tasks from >=71% to <25% after one game session
>=78% of players answer >=14 of 18 in-game formative questions correctly within a single 15-minute session
>=70% of enrolled students complete all three game chapters in a single sitting
>=80% of players accumulate >=5 unique concept cards in the Vocabulary panel by end of session
>=75% of students correctly apply Connextra user-story format in a 48-hour delayed written quiz

proposalState.narrativeTheme: In a rain-slicked neon city where critical software projects are failing because of poorly written requirements, the Runtime Analyst is a freelance systems analyst who must sprint, jump, and slide through three increasingly complex data districts to classify UML artefacts before they corrupt the city infrastructure. Every quiz gate tests the runner's ability to distinguish actors from use cases, include from extend, and valid Connextra user stories from vague system wishes — a mission structured across all six cognitive levels of Bloom Taxonomy (Remember through Create). The game is designed to satisfy all three psychological needs of Self-Determination Theory: autonomy is preserved through free lane choice and store customisation; competence is built through streak feedback, escalating chapter speed, and vocabulary growth; relatedness is cultivated through co-located classroom play and shared highscore comparison. Every mechanic is analysed through the Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics lens to ensure the felt experience of mastery and discovery aligns with learning intent at every chapter: quiz-gate mechanics produce tension-and-relief dynamics that yield the aesthetic of challenge and discovery, while word-cube collection produces assembly dynamics that yield the aesthetic of expression and accomplishment. Player motivation is balanced across the full HEXAD spectrum — Achiever 20%, Philanthropist 20%, Socialiser 20%, Free Spirit 15%, Player 15%, Disruptor 10% — so that every student personality profile finds a compelling engagement hook. Effectiveness is evaluated at all four tiers of Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation: satisfaction surveys at Level 1, pre/post accuracy gains at Level 2, transfer to lab use-case diagrams at Level 3, and cohort-level assessment improvement at Level 4. The compassion model applies throughout: losePoints=0 — every wrong answer surfaces an explanation and becomes a retrieval-practice opportunity, never a punitive dead end.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Players freely choose which lane to enter at every quiz gate — three options, one decision, no forced path. They decide when to open the Store, which skin to equip, and whether to activate the Starting Shield perk. Power-up usage timing is entirely player-controlled. The Vocabulary panel and Concept Guide can be consulted at will from the title screen or mid-session pause menu, giving players agency over their own scaffolding level.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Immediate post-gate explanations close the feedback loop within 300 ms so players can self-correct before the next challenge. The streak counter and XP bar provide continuous visible evidence of growing accuracy. Chapter speed escalation (355 to 735 px/s across three chapters) creates a calibrated difficulty curve that keeps players in the flow zone. The Vocabulary badge count offers a tangible proxy for accumulated knowledge, and the final Certificate screen consolidates the session's competence evidence in one place.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: In-class co-located play allows students to discuss gate choices aloud before the character reaches each question, creating shared decision-making moments. The local highscore panel on the Certificate screen invites informal peer comparison on the same device. Shared synthwave atmosphere and arcade aesthetic build a common experiential vocabulary across the cohort. The Vocabulary panel's concept cards use consistent terminology that reinforces the shared language established in lectures, deepening the sense of belonging to a community of practice.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 1 quiz gate (kind: actor-usecase)
conceptMappings.example: Gate prompt: 'Bakery stock system — which is the ACTOR?' Three lane labels: Bakery Owner / Track Orders / Inventory System. Correct: Bakery Owner.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 1 quiz gate (kind: actor-usecase / mixed)
conceptMappings.example: Gate prompt: 'Library delivery system — which is a USE CASE?' Correct lane: Online Book Borrow.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 2 quiz gate (kind: story) + word-cube sentence
conceptMappings.example: Gap-fill gate: 'As a ___, I want to request a book online...' plus Connextra sentence assembled from word cubes.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 2 quiz gate (kind: relationship)
conceptMappings.example: Scenario: 'Every registration must perform email verification.' Player must select include lane; extend lane is incorrect.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 2 quiz gate (kind: relationship)
conceptMappings.example: Scenario: 'Encrypt message only when sender activates privacy mode.' Player must select extend lane.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 3 quiz gate (kind: boundary)
conceptMappings.example: Prompt: 'Which item belongs INSIDE the system boundary?' Correct: a use case. Incorrect: an actor.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 2 quiz gate (kind: story — validity)
conceptMappings.example: 'The system shall process everything quickly' is invalid; player must select the lane labelled as the valid Connextra alternative.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Chapter 3 quiz gate (kind: mixed)
conceptMappings.example: Scenario: 'Login always logs the session; 2FA only when enabled.' Correct lane: include then extend.

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Three-lane endless runner (jump/slide/lane-switch), three-option quiz gates, word-cube sentence construction, five power-up types (Tablet 9s, Kalem 8s, Hologram 10s, Kuantum 9.5s, Kalkan single-use), three obstacle sub-types (blocker/hurdle/scanner), coin economy, streak multiplier, vocabulary persistence, chapter progression with speed escalation
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: Risk-reward tension at every quiz gate (correct lane = coins and XP; wrong lane = life lost); streak preservation drives accuracy over speed; coin resource management creates a save-vs-spend dilemma for skin and perk purchases; increasing chapter speed forces the player to rely on internalised rules rather than deliberate recall; vocabulary badge growth provides a tangible proxy for learning progress
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: Felt experience: mastery satisfaction as streak extends; discovery as vocabulary panel fills; sensation through synthwave neon visuals and retro-80s audio; challenge from escalating chapter speeds; expression through skin customisation; narrative purpose as the city's failing requirements systems are restored chapter by chapter

scenarios.title: Wrong Relationship Selected at Speed
scenarios.setup: Chapter 2, quiz gate presents a mandatory sub-flow scenario; player chooses the extend lane at high speed
scenarios.winCondition: Player reads the immediate explanation, retains life via Starting Shield perk or corrects choice on retry
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 — no score penalty applied; one life decremented and include/extend explanation displayed (compassion model: zero punitive)
scenarios.outcome: Player advances with reinforced understanding of mandatory vs conditional relationships; vocabulary card added for the missed concept

scenarios.title: Lives Depleted Before Chapter Target Reached
scenarios.setup: Player exhausts all three lives before accumulating the required correct answers for the chapter
scenarios.winCondition: Player selects Restart from the game-over screen and applies learned explanations in a new run
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 — prior vocabulary cards and coins are retained; only the run resets (compassion model: failure becomes a retrieval-practice opportunity)
scenarios.outcome: Retry run benefits from accumulated vocabulary; player approaches familiar question types with corrected mental model

scenarios.title: Power-Up Depleted at Critical Gate
scenarios.setup: Chapter 3, player's Tablet power-up expires mid-run just before a complex mixed-relationship gate
scenarios.winCondition: Player correctly identifies the include/extend relationship from memory alone and passes the gate
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 — wrong answer triggers explanation overlay; player continues with one fewer life (compassion model)
scenarios.outcome: Demonstrates transfer from supported to unsupported recall; reinforces deep schema formation for mixed-relationship modelling

scenarios.title: Vocabulary Panel as Exam Revision Tool
scenarios.setup: Student opens the Vocabulary panel after completing all three chapters with eight concept cards collected
scenarios.winCondition: Student uses concept cards as reference during the 48-hour delayed quiz and scores above the 75% threshold
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 — incomplete vocabulary is not penalised; student encouraged to replay to fill remaining cards
scenarios.outcome: Demonstrates persistent learning artefact value beyond the game session itself

frs.description: System shall log each player quiz answer, word-pickup event, coin transaction, power-up activation, and chapter completion within a client-side audit-grade event record stored in localStorage key shadow_analyst_log.
System shall render a three-lane endless-runner on HTML5 Canvas and respond to left/right lane-switch, jump, and slide inputs via keyboard arrow keys, swipe gestures, and on-screen pad buttons.

System shall display a three-option quiz gate at configurable intervals drawn from RUNNER_QUESTIONS; each gate lane shall be labelled with one answer option; correct lane entry shall award coins and XP and trigger a vocabulary entry insertion.
System shall provide immediate feedback within 300 ms after each quiz gate with a correct/incorrect indicator and a one-sentence explanation drawn from the question's explain property.

System shall implement three obstacle sub-types: blocker (avoided by lane-switch), hurdle (avoided by jump), scanner (avoided by slide); collision caused by incorrect response shall decrement the player's lives counter by one.

System shall spawn word-cube objects from LESSON_SENTENCES on the track; collecting all cubes of the active sentence in correct sequence shall complete that sentence and insert its vocabulary entry into localStorage.
System shall persist wallet state (coins, inventory, equipped skin, highscores) in localStorage key shadow_analyst_wallet and restore it fully on every page load without data loss.

System shall persist vocabulary state (concept, definition, note, chapter, date) in localStorage key shadow_analyst_vocab using duplicate-safe insertion keyed on concept name; the HUD vocabulary badge shall reflect the current entry count in real time.

System shall advance the player to the next chapter when their correct-answer count reaches the chapter targetAnswers value (Chapter 1: 5, Chapter 2: 6, Chapter 3: 7) and display a chapter summary screen before continuing.

System shall provide a Store screen accessible from the title and pause menu where players may purchase and equip four character skins (0 / 500 / 1000 / 1500 coins) and one Starting Shield perk (2000 coins); purchase buttons shall be disabled when the player's coin balance is insufficient.

System shall spawn five power-up types on the track with randomised lane placement and timing: Tablet (9 s buff), Kalem (8 s buff), Hologram (10 s buff), Kuantum (9.5 s buff), Kalkan (single-use shield); effects shall be applied immediately upon collection.

System shall loop a synthwave background audio track; a mute/unmute toggle button shall remain accessible at all times including during active gameplay; audio shall start only after the first user interaction to comply with browser autoplay policies.
System shall allow the player to pause and resume the run at any time via keyboard ESC or an on-screen button; the pause screen shall offer Continue and Restart actions.
System shall shuffle RUNNER_QUESTIONS within each chapter per session so that question order varies on every run and no question immediately repeats.

System shall display a final Certificate screen upon completing Chapter 3, showing session statistics (score, correct answers, coins earned, elapsed time), a link to the Vocabulary panel, and a local highscore leaderboard.

System shall provide a Concept Guide panel accessible from the title screen listing plain-language definitions for the six core SAD concepts: Actor, Use Case, User Story, include relationship, extend relationship, and System Boundary.
