proposalState.gameDuration: 15 minutes per session: 1 min title screen and optional Concept Guide review + 3 min Chapter 1 run (Actor and Use Case) + 30 s Chapter 1 sentence exercise + 4 min Chapter 2 run (User Story and Relationships) + 30 s Chapter 2 sentence exercise + 5 min Chapter 3 run (Full Use Case Model) + 30 s Chapter 3 sentence exercise + 1 min Certificate screen review. Store visits are optional and additional.

sessionSteps.name: Title screen and onboarding
sessionSteps.description: Player views title hero, reads game premise, optionally opens Concept Guide for six core SAD definitions or reviews Vocabulary panel from a prior session

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 1 start — Actor and Use Case
sessionSteps.description: Chapter onboarding screen displays chapter title, focus concepts, and target correct-answer count (5); run begins at base speed 355 px/s

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 1 run
sessionSteps.description: Player runs three lanes, dodges blocker/hurdle/scanner obstacles, passes quiz gates on actor and use-case identification, collects word cubes and coins

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 1 sentence exercise
sessionSteps.description: Chapter summary screen presents a sentence-building task using collected word cubes to complete an Actor or Use Case definition; vocabulary card added on completion

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 2 start — User Story and Relationships
sessionSteps.description: Speed increases to base 405 px/s; focus shifts to Connextra user stories and include/extend relationship selection

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 2 run
sessionSteps.description: Six correct answers required; quiz gates introduce user-story gap-fill and mandatory-vs-conditional relationship scenarios

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 2 sentence exercise
sessionSteps.description: Two sentence-building tasks: one for user-story format, one for the include/extend rule; vocabulary cards added on completion

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 3 start — Full Use Case Model
sessionSteps.description: Speed increases to base 455 px/s; mixed-model questions combining actors, use cases, relationships, and system boundary introduced

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 3 run
sessionSteps.description: Seven correct answers required; gates include boundary identification, combined include-then-extend scenarios, and stakeholder-system separation tasks

sessionSteps.name: Chapter 3 sentence exercise
sessionSteps.description: Two sentence-building tasks covering system boundary definition and full modelling workflow summary; vocabulary cards added

sessionSteps.name: Certificate screen
sessionSteps.description: Player reviews session statistics (score, correct answers, coins earned, elapsed time), opens Vocabulary panel for all collected concept cards, views local highscore

sessionSteps.name: Store visit (optional)
sessionSteps.description: Player spends accumulated coins on character skins or Starting Shield perk in preparation for next session

proposalState.logPrivacy: No personally identifiable information is collected or transmitted. Game state — coin balance, inventory, equipped skin, highscores, and vocabulary entries — is stored exclusively in the player's own browser localStorage under two keys (shadow_analyst_wallet and shadow_analyst_vocab). No analytics scripts, tracking pixels, or external API calls are made beyond loading Google Fonts and the static audio file from their original CDN locations. The application is fully compliant with GDPR and KVKK in the zero-processing category.
proposalState.dataCollectionPlan: Pilot study only: voluntary participants provide written informed consent before the session. An 18-item anonymous paper pre-test is administered before play and an identical post-test within 24 hours. A 48-hour delayed quiz assesses Connextra format retention. A Likert satisfaction survey (5 items) is collected immediately after the Certificate screen. No in-game telemetry is transmitted; the instructor may optionally ask students to share a screenshot of their Certificate screen score for class discussion — participation in sharing is voluntary and grade-neutral. All paper instruments are anonymised by student-chosen code before analysis.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Mandatory pre-session instructor briefing on game objectives and controls; facilitator script provided; pilot run conducted one week before class deployment
Chocolate-broccoli risk: concept explanations embedded in every gate response; Bloom L2-L6 tasks require active use of learned terms; Concept Guide and Vocabulary panel keep learning visible
Ethics risk — pilot participation is voluntary, nota-neutral, and covered by informed consent; no PII collected; GDPR and KVKK compliance verified before deployment
localStorage cleared by student risk — bilingual toast warning on first load; future JSON export/import option planned; game state recovery from in-memory fallback
Browser autoplay policy blocks background music — music starts only after first user interaction; muted-by-default fallback silently applied; mute toggle always accessible
11-member team coordination risk — single HTML file minimises merge conflicts; weekly standup with Project Lead (Caner Caliskan); design/content/QA/presentation sub-groups with clear ownership
Question difficulty miscalibration — Bloom-levelled question bank reviewed by instructor before deployment; pilot session provides calibration data for difficulty adjustments
Colour-blind accessibility — obstacle types differentiated by distinct shapes (laser barrier / overhead scanner / full blocker) in addition to colour; WCAG 2.1 AA contrast verified
Low-end mobile performance — DPR-aware Canvas scaling applied; parallax layers reduced on small viewports; 30 FPS minimum target validated on 4-year-old mid-range device
Asset loading failure (images/MP3 not co-located) — game degrades gracefully to Canvas-only procedural visuals and silent mode; no white screen or crash on missing assets

supportTeam.responsibility: Content validation, pilot test supervision, pre/post quiz administration, Kirkpatrick L3-L4 assessment coordination
Sprint coordination, final integration, JSON proposal, presentation lead
Synthwave visual identity, Canvas rendering layer, parallax scene, character skins
RUNNER_QUESTIONS bank, LESSON_SENTENCES, concept explanations, Concept Guide text
Cross-browser testing, performance profiling, accessibility audit, autoplay policy validation
Demo video, slide deck, rubric documentation, delivery rehearsal

proposalState.facilitatorRole: During in-class pilot sessions the instructor acts as facilitator: projects the game on screen, divides students into groups of four to five for rotating play, pauses after each quiz gate explanation to invite class discussion, and uses wrong-answer explanations as teaching moments rather than corrections. After the Certificate screen the facilitator leads a structured debrief referencing the Vocabulary panel and connecting game scenarios to the lab use-case diagram task due the following week. The synthwave arcade atmosphere is used intentionally to lower performance anxiety; the facilitator reinforces the compassion model by reminding students that wrong answers carry zero score penalty and exist solely to surface the explanation.
proposalState.wf_dash: Top HUD bar: chapter label | score | XP bar | coin count | streak chip (amber) | lives icons | active power-up timer. Top-right: Vocabulary button with badge count + mute toggle. Centre: HTML5 Canvas — three-lane perspective track with synthwave sunset background image, parallax cyberpunk city silhouette, neon street lamps, and rain particles. Obstacle types visually distinct by silhouette: laser barrier (low, hurdle), overhead scanner (tall, slide), full blocker (wide, lane-switch). Quiz gate appears as three labelled arches spanning all lanes with prompt text above. Bottom (mobile only): four-button control pad (left / right / jump / slide) sized >=44 px with safe-area inset padding.
proposalState.wf_asset: Character sprites: four skin variants (Neon Classic #00f5ff, Cyber Gold #ffe066, Crimson Shadow #ff2e54, Poison Green #6affc8) with idle/run/jump/slide animation frames. Five power-up pickups: diamond shape with rotating outer ring, glow shadow, and two-letter label (TA / KA / KK / HO / KU). Word-cube prefab: frosted glass card with concept word in Chakra Petch font, cyan glow border. Two external image assets: synthwave sunset/grid/mountains background JPG; cyberpunk city silhouette JPG (background-removal processed). One external audio asset: monume-synthwave-retro-80s MP3 (looped). Five Google Fonts: Orbitron (headings), Audiowide (HUD values), Rajdhani (body), Chakra Petch (labels), VT323 (retro decorative).
proposalState.wf_portfolio: Store screen: wallet balance chip top-right; four skin cards (icon + name + price + Buy/Equipped button) in a 2x2 grid; one perk card below. Vocabulary panel: stats chips (concept count + chapter count); scrollable grid of concept cards each showing concept name, definition, note, chapter tag, and acquisition date; Reset Vocabulary button with confirmation. Concept Guide panel: six concept tiles each showing term, one-sentence definition, and an example drawn from the game's question bank. Certificate screen: session stats row (score / correct answers / coins / time); Open Vocabulary button; local highscore list; Play Again button. Chapter onboarding screen: chapter eyebrow + title + two-line focus + Continue button. Pause screen: Paused eyebrow + two action buttons (Continue / Restart).
proposalState.inScope: Single HTML-file browser game (Canvas + Vanilla JS) plus two image assets and one MP3; 3 chapters x 18 quiz questions x 7 sentence-building exercises; 4 skins + 1 perk store; 5 power-up types; 3 obstacle sub-types; two localStorage persistence keys (wallet + vocabulary); English-language content; Vocabulary and Concept Guide panels; synthwave visual and audio identity; in-class pilot test; presentation and JSON proposal. Compassion model: losePoints=0 across all scenarios; no punitive scoring; every failure state surfaces an explanation and becomes a learning opportunity.
proposalState.outScope: Server-side multiplayer, real-time global leaderboard, user account and login system, payment integration, multi-language support, app-store publication, AI-driven personalised question generation, instructor analytics dashboard with per-student telemetry, VR/AR modes, LMS integration (SCORM/xAPI), custom sound-effect library beyond the included synthwave track.
proposalState.whyMotivation: Passive lecture delivery leaves 71% of COM4061 students below threshold on UML modelling tasks after three terms of instruction. Game-based learning literature (Hamari et al. 2014; Sailer et al. 2017) demonstrates that short-cycle formative feedback, intrinsic reward structures, and low-stakes repeated practice measurably improve conceptual retention. Runtime Analyst delivers all three within a zero-install, single-HTML-file format that requires no accounts, no servers, and no setup time — removing every practical barrier to in-class and out-of-class use. The synthwave aesthetic creates an emotionally engaging environment that sustains attention beyond the lecture slot, and the persistent Vocabulary panel converts a transient play session into a lasting study resource.
proposalState.howEthics: Data minimisation: no personal data collected; only anonymous game state (coins, vocabulary, highscore) stored in the player's own browser localStorage; zero external transmission. Voluntary participation: pilot enrolment is opt-in with no grade consequence. Informed consent: purpose, duration, and data handling disclosed in writing before any pilot session. Accessibility: obstacle types coded by shape as well as colour; audio mutable; mobile-friendly layout with minimum 44 px touch targets. Content ethics: no violent, discriminatory, or gender-stereotyped content; synthwave theme is stylised and non-graphic. Anti-addiction design: sessions target 15 minutes and terminate naturally at the Certificate screen; no infinite-loop mechanic designed to maximise time-on-device. Intellectual property: Google Fonts used under SIL Open Font Licence; background audio attributed and used within educational fair-use scope; all game code original.

teams.title: Logic Path: The Activity Diagram Labyrinth

ideaBrief.title: Logic Path
ideaBrief.domain: Software engineering education / UML activity diagrams
ideaBrief.audience: CS and software engineering undergraduates in COM4061 who are learning how to read, interpret, and construct activity diagrams
ideaBrief.coreMetaphor: A logic labyrinth where each movement decision becomes a visible part of an activity diagram
ideaBrief.successVision: After three guided sessions, learners can navigate a scenario, explain why a path branches or rejoins, and assemble a correct activity diagram without the experience feeling like a quiz.

proposalState.projectTitle: Logic Path: The Activity Diagram Labyrinth
proposalState.organisation: Istanbul Kultur University, Department of Computer Engineering
proposalState.domain: Software engineering education / UML activity diagrams
proposalState.deliverableArea: Browser-based educational game for learning, constructing, and interpreting UML activity diagrams
proposalState.problemStatement: A needs analysis with 60 COM4061 students at Istanbul Kultur University showed that 64% could recognize a start or end node in isolation, but 58% mixed up decision and merge nodes, 61% could not explain fork/join concurrency, and 71% said activity diagrams felt more like exam syntax than process reasoning. The instructor also asked for a format that does not feel like Kahoot, homework, or a timed test. Logic Path responds with a compassion model where losePoints=0 and no punitive failure loop is used, so students learn activity-diagram logic by making choices in a maze and watching a live diagram appear from those decisions.
proposalState.targetPopulation: Approximately 60 CS/SE undergraduates enrolled in COM4061 at Istanbul Kultur University, mainly second- and third-year students aged 19-23 who need practice with UML activity diagrams.
successCriteria: Increase post-test accuracy in identifying UML activity diagram symbols by >=30% over baseline.
Achieve >=80% correct completion on scenario-to-diagram assembly tasks by the end of the third guided level.
>=75% of learners score at least 70% on a delayed 48-hour retention check covering decision, merge, fork, and join nodes.
>=60% of pilot learners voluntarily return for a second play session within 7 days, indicating that the experience feels like a game rather than homework.
Reduce facilitator interventions for symbol confusion by >=40% between the first tutorial session and the final capstone run.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Recall the meaning of core UML activity diagram symbols, including initial node, final node, action, decision, merge, fork, and join, after the tutorial levels.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Symbol legend exploration, entrance-room discovery tasks, and first-level maze prompts.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Pre/post recognition check with symbol-to-function matching items.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Explain how flow moves through an activity diagram and why a branch, merge, fork, or join is used in a given scenario.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Branching labyrinth rooms with live diagram updates and short reflection prompts.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Short explanation task and instructor rubric scored within 24 hours.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Apply activity diagram symbols correctly while navigating a scenario and placing missing UML tiles to complete the intended process flow.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Build-a-bridge levels where the learner selects the correct node or connector to reopen the path.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Gameplay trace review and accuracy score on scenario completion.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Analyse multi-step scenarios to identify where decisions, alternate flows, and concurrent actions should appear in the diagram.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Decision-chain and fork-join labyrinth levels with alternate valid paths to compare.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Scenario analysis worksheet and event-log based rubric.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Evaluate whether a generated activity diagram correctly matches a written system scenario and justify which parts need revision.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Diagram review levels where the player inspects a generated flow against a scenario brief.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Rubric-scored critique explaining mismatches and corrections.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Create a complete activity diagram from a realistic case by navigating the labyrinth, selecting the correct nodes, and assembling the final flow.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Capstone scenario in which the full diagram is constructed from player decisions across several rooms.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Capstone diagram judged for correctness, completeness, and reasoning quality.

proposalState.narrativeTheme: Logic Path takes place inside the Process Vault, a shifting system world where unfinished workflows become physical labyrinths. Players enter as apprentice analysts trying to restore order to broken scenarios from the System Analysis and Design course. Each doorway represents a condition, each corridor represents a flow, and every room the learner unlocks reveals another part of the hidden process. The game is designed around Self-Determination Theory so learners keep control over route choice, hint use, replay, and pacing; around Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics so the maze, diagram panel, and feedback loops feel like one coherent play experience; around Bloom Taxonomy so tasks rise from remembering symbols to creating a complete activity diagram; around HEXAD so achievers, free spirits, players, social learners, and other profiles all find a reason to stay engaged; and around Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation so the proposal measures reaction, learning, behavior, and course-level results. The conflict is not beating an enemy or answering quiz pop-ups, but escaping a confusing process world by understanding how flow really works. As the learner moves, the diagram terminal translates the journey into visible logic, proving that an activity diagram is not static homework but a living map of decisions, branches, merges, and concurrency.
proposalState.gameOverview: Logic Path is a browser-based educational game for System Analysis and Design in which the labyrinth is the input and the activity diagram is the output. Players move through a coordinate-grid maze, choose paths at condition gates, repair missing flow segments with UML tiles, and watch a live diagram panel render their decisions in real time. Environmental interaction replaces popup-heavy recall so the experience does not feel like homework or a Kahoot clone. Figma-designed layouts, SVG UML assets, and JSON-driven level logic keep the implementation feasible while the learning design stays aligned with Self-Determination Theory, Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics, Bloom Taxonomy, HEXAD, Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation, and a white-hat Octalysis balance.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Learners decide which branch to test first, when to open optional hints, whether to replay a room immediately or continue, and how to place missing UML tiles during bridge-repair moments. The game guides the learner, but it never locks every decision behind one rigid answer order.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Competence grows through immediate visual feedback because every correct movement adds to the live diagram, every mistake is explained at the exact point of confusion, and later levels raise complexity from simple symbol recall to branching and concurrency. Progress feels earned because the player can see the logic getting better on screen.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: Relatedness is supported through facilitator-led debriefs, optional peer comparison of solved diagrams, respectful alias-based rankings, and shared discussion about why one path was better than another. Learners are reminded that they are exploring system logic together instead of being judged in isolation.

rewardStructure.elite: Elite tier recognizes learners who complete the capstone labyrinth with high diagram accuracy, strong concurrency reasoning, and low hint dependence. Rewards include the Logic Architect badge, an advanced challenge pack, and showcase placement in the course gallery.
rewardStructure.professional: Professional tier recognizes steady learners who solve branching and scenario levels correctly even if they still need light support on advanced concurrency. Rewards include the Flow Builder badge, facilitator feedback highlights, and access to extra replay missions.
rewardStructure.access: Access tier rewards consistent participation, exploration, and improvement for every learner. Rewards include symbol legend upgrades, starter achievement badges, gentle XP boosts, and replay tokens that keep progress accessible without punishing mistakes.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Entrance portal and exit gate markers that open and close the run
Walkable task tiles that stamp action boxes into the live diagram
Forked hallway with a condition gate that forces a branch choice
Rejoin chamber that collapses alternate paths into one corridor
Dual-lane puzzle that requires two parallel actions before the door opens
Capstone maze where a written case is converted into a full rendered flow

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Core mechanics include grid-based movement, condition gates, environmental puzzle-solving, UML tile placement, live diagram rendering, replay, progressive unlocks, hints, points, badges, and a light leaderboard that supports progress without dominating the experience.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: These mechanics create experimentation, visible cause-and-effect reasoning, comparison between alternate paths, and iterative refinement. Learners move, see a diagram update, notice mismatches, and immediately revise their understanding through replay rather than through punitive failure.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: The intended feeling is curiosity first, then clarity, mastery, and confidence. Players should feel like they are uncovering process logic inside a game world, not sitting for an exam, while the growing diagram gives a satisfying sense of order emerging from exploration.

scenarios.title: Symbols in the Entrance Wing
scenarios.setup: The learner enters the tutorial wing of the labyrinth where each room represents one basic symbol. The path is simple, but the player must match entrances, exits, and action tiles so the diagram panel begins to mirror the route.
scenarios.winCondition: The learner reaches the first exit after placing the correct initial node, action node, and final node sequence with at least 80% accuracy.
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 - the compassion model keeps all progress, shows a friendly symbol hint, and lets the learner retry the room with no punitive score loss.
scenarios.outcome: The learner unlocks the legend overlay, stores the first valid mini-diagram, and gains confidence with the basic symbol set.
scenarios.neutralCondition: If the learner reaches the exit but mislabels one symbol, the system keeps the path, highlights the mismatch, and offers a quick correction pass.

scenarios.title: Condition Gate Junction
scenarios.setup: The learner reaches a branching corridor guarded by a condition such as whether a user is logged in. The maze now behaves like a decision node, and the live diagram must show both the branch and the later merge correctly.
scenarios.winCondition: The learner chooses the valid branch, reaches the merge chamber, and produces a correct decision-to-merge flow before the checkpoint closes.
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 - no punitive penalty is applied; the learner keeps the discovered rooms, receives a compassion model explanation of the wrong branch, and retries from the decision gate.
scenarios.outcome: The system records a working example of alternate flow and merge behavior, and the learner earns branching mastery progress.
scenarios.neutralCondition: If the learner identifies the decision correctly but misses the best merge connection, the game accepts partial progress and marks the merge node for guided revision.

scenarios.title: Parallel Workshop Doors
scenarios.setup: A heavy door can only open when two actions happen in parallel, so the learner must understand that one flow splits into concurrent paths and joins again later. The room teaches fork and join through environment design instead of quiz prompts.
scenarios.winCondition: The learner triggers both parallel tasks, returns them to the join chamber, and generates a valid fork-join pattern in the output diagram.
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 - the compassion model preserves the learner's valid path fragments, explains why concurrency was missing, and offers a replay with extra visual guidance instead of punishment.
scenarios.outcome: The learner unlocks advanced UML pieces, stores a concurrency example, and becomes ready for a real-world scenario case.
scenarios.neutralCondition: If one branch is completed correctly but the join is malformed, the game saves the better branch, flags the join, and invites a focused repair attempt.

scenarios.title: Student Registration Process
scenarios.setup: The capstone level presents a realistic university registration scenario with validation checks, alternate approval paths, and a concurrent payment-confirmation segment. The learner must navigate the labyrinth so the final output becomes a coherent activity diagram for the case.
scenarios.winCondition: The learner assembles a complete diagram that matches the written scenario, including the correct start, actions, decisions, merges, concurrency segment, and final state.
scenarios.loseCondition: losePoints=0 - the learner receives non-punitive review notes, keeps completed sections, and can revise the capstone diagram through replay without public embarrassment.
scenarios.outcome: A polished final diagram is stored for reflection, progress rewards are granted, and the learner demonstrates readiness for course assignments.
scenarios.neutralCondition: If the main flow is correct but one optional branch is weak, the capstone is accepted as partial success and the system recommends one replay mission for improvement.

gamePhases.name: Observe the map
gamePhases.description: The learner reads the scenario brief, reviews the symbol legend, and enters the coordinate-grid maze while the live diagram panel starts empty.
gamePhases.diagramFocus: Initial node recognition and path expectations

gamePhases.name: Navigate and decide
gamePhases.description: The learner moves through rooms, chooses branches at condition gates, and starts seeing each action appear as structured diagram output.
gamePhases.diagramFocus: Action nodes, decision nodes, and merge logic

gamePhases.name: Repair and synchronize
gamePhases.description: The learner places missing UML tiles, tests concurrency rooms, and checks whether the generated diagram still matches the environment.

gamePhases.name: Reflect and replay
gamePhases.description: The learner reviews the completed flow, studies hints or corrections, and replays specific rooms to improve accuracy without losing progress.

frs.description: System shall present a grid-based labyrinth where movement through rooms and corridors represents activity flow decisions.
System shall render a live activity diagram panel that converts validated movement and tile placement into initial, action, decision, merge, fork, join, and final symbols.
System shall ask the learner to choose or confirm conditions at decision gates and reflect the selected branch in the diagram output.
System shall provide a toolbox of UML shapes and connectors so learners can repair broken paths or complete missing process segments during higher levels.
System shall display a symbol legend, scenario brief, hint button, and supportive feedback layer without interrupting core gameplay.
System shall support replay, progressive level unlocking, partial-progress saving, and facilitator-ready summaries after each level.

System shall log each movement event, branch choice, node placement, hint request, validation result, replay action, and completion milestone in an audit-grade event record for learning analytics and facilitator review.

proposalState.gameDuration: 45 minutes total: 5 minutes orientation, 8 minutes symbol discovery, 10 minutes branching play, 10 minutes fork-join puzzle solving, 7 minutes capstone scenario assembly, and 5 minutes reflection.

sessionSteps.name: Orientation and objective briefing
sessionSteps.description: The facilitator introduces the Logic Path story, explains that the labyrinth is the input and the activity diagram is the output, and confirms that the session is non-punitive.

sessionSteps.name: Symbol tutorial run
sessionSteps.description: Learners enter the entrance wing, review the legend, and complete the initial path that teaches start, action, and end nodes through direct play.

sessionSteps.name: Decision path challenge
sessionSteps.description: Learners reach branching corridors, choose among condition gates, and compare how different routes generate different decision and merge structures in the diagram panel.

sessionSteps.name: Parallel path challenge
sessionSteps.description: Learners solve the fork-join puzzle by coordinating two required actions and inspecting how concurrency is represented in the output diagram.

sessionSteps.name: Real scenario assembly
sessionSteps.description: Learners complete the capstone registration scenario and use the maze plus shape toolbox to assemble a coherent activity diagram from a realistic case.

sessionSteps.name: Reflection and score review
sessionSteps.description: The facilitator leads a short debrief, reviews hints and replay patterns, and connects the finished diagrams to course assignments and UML practice.

proposalState.logPrivacy: The platform records movement logs, branch choices, node placements, hint usage, replay attempts, validation events, and completion summaries under pseudonymised learner identifiers. Consent is collected before analytics are enabled, direct PII is excluded from routine reports, and stored data follows GDPR and KVKK privacy expectations.
proposalState.dataCollectionPlan: Data collection combines pre/post knowledge checks, delayed 48-hour retention checks, event logs, hint usage counts, replay frequency, completion times, short usability feedback, and facilitator observations. JSON-based level traces record which coordinates triggered which diagram changes while minimising personal data and protecting privacy through pseudonymisation and access controls.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Prototype the movement-to-diagram mapping early with a limited symbol set before building advanced levels and concurrency rooms.
tuslerRisks.preventive: Freeze the mapping rules for initial, action, decision, merge, fork, join, and final nodes before visual polish begins.
tuslerRisks.responsive: If desynchronisation appears during pilot play, pause the affected level, show a fallback static diagram preview, and log the error for rapid repair.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Keep the maze readable and educational by limiting early complexity, testing room layouts with students, and balancing challenge with instructional clarity.
tuslerRisks.preventive: Use low-fidelity playtests to verify that each room teaches one new idea before the next complexity layer is introduced.
tuslerRisks.responsive: If learners become lost more than they learn, simplify the map, shorten dead ends, and move difficult logic into later replay levels.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Pair every major maze interaction with immediate diagram feedback and concise explanations so the mapping between path and UML output stays visible.
tuslerRisks.preventive: Test comprehension by asking pilot users to explain what a branch or join means after each major room.
tuslerRisks.responsive: If players solve the maze but still misread the diagram, add stronger overlays, slower playback of the generated flow, and a guided comparison view.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Protect the game feel by replacing most quiz pop-ups with environmental interaction, playful gates, and diagram-building actions.
tuslerRisks.preventive: Review every new level against the rule that it must feel like exploration first and assessment second.
tuslerRisks.responsive: If students describe the pilot as homework-like, remove recall-heavy prompts and rebalance the level toward movement, discovery, and visible output.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Stage the UML symbol set carefully so learners meet basic nodes before decisions, merges, and concurrency structures.
tuslerRisks.preventive: Use a guided legend, a short tutorial wing, and scaffolded hints before the capstone scenario opens.
tuslerRisks.responsive: If symbol overload appears in logs or feedback, add one more practice room and reduce the number of new nodes introduced per level.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Keep the interface lightweight, responsive, and tested on the common browsers and laptop resolutions used in class.
tuslerRisks.preventive: Run classroom device checks on the labyrinth panel, live diagram panel, and support rail before pilot launch.
tuslerRisks.responsive: If layout issues appear, switch to the simplified three-panel fallback layout and patch the affected CSS before the next session.

proposalState.facilitatorRole: The facilitator acts as a non-playing guide who introduces the scenario, clarifies how labyrinth actions map to diagram logic, monitors timing, and supports stuck learners with context-sensitive hints. The facilitator does not turn mistakes into punishment, does not shame low performers, and uses the logs only to support reflection, progress tracking, and evaluation.
proposalState.wf_dash: Main gameplay wireframe: a wide labyrinth panel sits on the left as the primary input space, while a live activity diagram panel sits on the right as the output space. A top strip shows level name, objective, timer, and current symbol focus. A bottom rail contains movement help, UML tile tools, hint access, and replay controls. The layout keeps the learner's path and the generated diagram visible at the same time so causality is always clear.
proposalState.wf_asset: Learning support wireframe: the screen is split into a scenario brief card, a symbol legend with UML meanings, and a feedback zone that explains why the last branch, merge, fork, or join was accepted or revised. A compact history strip shows recently generated nodes, and a support button opens guided examples without removing the learner from the current level. This surface helps the learner understand the logic behind the maze instead of treating the task like a quiz.
proposalState.wf_portfolio: Progress dashboard wireframe: the header shows learner alias, completed levels, diagram accuracy, replay count, and earned badges. The main area presents level-by-level completion cards, saved diagrams, branching accuracy, concurrency accuracy, and recommended replay targets. A reflection panel highlights delayed-retention reminders, facilitator notes, and next-step suggestions so improvement feels personal, visible, and supportive rather than punitive.
proposalState.inScope: In scope: a browser-based educational game where the labyrinth is the input and the activity diagram is the output, using a compassion model with losePoints=0, no punitive scoring, guided hints, replay, progressive unlocks, and lightweight analytics for COM4061 learners.
proposalState.outScope: Out of scope: Kahoot-style quiz clones, native mobile delivery, public shaming leaderboards, harsh elimination mechanics, and large-scale multiplayer competition. The proposal intentionally avoids punitive embarrassment loops, permanent failure states, and designs that make the activity feel like an exam instead of a game.
proposalState.whyMotivation: Why this motivates learners: Logic Path turns abstract process logic into visible movement, immediate diagram output, and achievable mastery steps. Exploration, discovery, and meaningful repair actions keep the experience playful, while the compassion model keeps losePoints=0 and no punitive feedback at the center so learners stay willing to experiment, replay, and improve.
proposalState.howEthics: How ethics are protected: the platform limits data collection to what is necessary for evaluation, uses pseudonymised analytics, follows GDPR and KVKK expectations, offers accessible interaction patterns, and frames support through a compassion model with losePoints=0 and no punitive public shaming. Learners receive consent information, readable feedback, and multiple support routes so the system remains fair, respectful, and inclusive.

proposalState.problemStatement: COM4061 System Analysis & Design students demonstrate a persistent gap between theoretical knowledge and applied system thinking: 58% fail to correctly decompose a system into components on unseen exam questions, and fewer than 40% can accurately model a multi-step process flow without scaffolding (departmental assessment data, 2025). Existing coursework lacks authentic task-dependency simulations — students learn individual diagrams in isolation but cannot chain them into a coherent analysis workflow. Island Escape addresses this by embedding all core SAD concepts inside a structured survival simulation: players must decompose the island system, model resource flows, identify dependencies, and plan an escape — mirroring real system analysis projects at every step. The game eliminates passive reception of theory by forcing students to apply SAD tools under resource constraints and causal feedback, producing a measurable learning gain in both process modelling accuracy and dependency analysis.
inScope: 12 structured missions mapped to COM4061 weekly chapters; Dependency lock system (mission unlock based on artefact validation); Automated artefact validation engine (component diagram, process flow, use case); Resource tracking system (Food, Tools, Materials); NPC dialogue system (structured mission briefings and fragmented requirements); Artefact export package (ZIP/JSON) for instructor submission; Tutorial mission (Mission 0) with guided component canvas; XP and badge system; Progress auto-save to localStorage
outScope: Real-time multiplayer (Phase 2); AI-generated adaptive missions (Phase 2); Mobile native app (browser-only in Phase 1); LMS/Moodle deep integration (Phase 2 — artefact export covers Phase 1 grading); Voice-narrated NPC dialogue (Phase 2); Instructor artefact dashboard (Phase 2 — manual review of exported packages in Phase 1)

sixDim.what:
🗺️ Quests / Missions
The game is structured around story-driven missions where players help different characters solve problems. Each quest teaches a system analysis concept through interactive puzzles, making progression feel purposeful and narrative-focused.

📈 Levels & Progression
Progression is tied to repairing the spaceship and unlocking new areas rather than simple experience points. Each completed challenge gives meaningful rewards such as ship parts, map knowledge, or new mechanics, reinforcing player growth.

▓ Progress Bars
The oxygen meter creates urgency and encourages efficient decision-making during exploration. Players can visually track survival status, which increases tension and engagement without overwhelming the gameplay.

⚔️ Challenges
Puzzle difficulty gradually increases throughout the game to maintain player interest and competence. Challenges focus on observation, logic, and system-thinking instead of combat, supporting the educational purpose of the game.

💬 Immediate Feedback
Players instantly see the results of their actions through environmental reactions, dialogue changes, and puzzle outcomes. This feedback loop helps players learn system analysis concepts naturally through experimentation.

🗺️ Game Board / Map
The map functions as both a navigation tool and a visual representation of system diagrams. Players learn to interpret paths and connections while making strategic exploration choices, integrating educational content into gameplay.

🎭 Roles / Characters
Characters such as the monkey, turtle engineer, flower, and villagers each represent different system design concepts and learning experiences. Their unique personalities make educational tasks feel more immersive and emotionally engaging.
sixDim.why: MDA Mechanics: task-based progression, resource constraints, dependency locking. MDA Dynamics: planning under scarcity, cascading task effects, bottleneck discovery. MDA Aesthetic: Mastery + Discovery + Narrative immersion. SDT Autonomy: multiple valid task sequences that still satisfy dependency rules. SDT Competence: visible mastery progression through mission completion. SDT Relatedness: NPC characters provide structured social context. Flow: difficulty ramps from single-dependency missions (Zone 1) to multi-chain parallel dependencies (Zone 5).
sixDim.who: Achievers 40% (complete all 12 missions with full component maps); Explorers 35% (discover alternative task sequences, optional NPC lore fragments); Free Spirits 25% (minimalist escape — fewest resources used). No Killer/competitive mechanics — this is a single-player structured simulation.
sixDim.when: We did online meetings once or twice a week
sixDim.how: Anti-shortcut safeguards: each mission requires a submitted SAD artefact (use case, component diagram, process model) that is automatically checked against a reference schema before XP is awarded — students cannot "click through" without producing correct structural output. Resource constraints prevent brute-force attempts. Dependency locks prevent out-of-order play. All mechanics are rule-based; no random number generation or luck-based outcomes.
sixDim.where: Single-file browser application (HTML5 + Vanilla JS). Zero installation. Works on university lab PCs and student laptops. No backend required for core gameplay — progress saved in localStorage, exportable as JSON artefact for instructor review.

proposalState.narrativeTheme: A research flight carrying a group of system analysts has crash-landed on an uncharted island. The only way to escape is to solve the island as a system — decompose its components, model its resource flows, identify the dependencies between survival tasks, and engineer a complete escape plan. Every island zone is a system waiting to be analysed. Every NPC is a stakeholder with fragmented information. Your escape is your final deliverable.
proposalState.characters: Players take the role of Zyx-7, an alien explorer who survives using nitrogen instead of oxygen. Stranded on Earth after a spaceship crash, the player must explore unfamiliar environments, help local creatures, and repair the spacecraft while learning system analysis concepts through problem-solving.
proposalState.storyWorld: The game takes place in a mysterious and colorful world filled with forests, caves, villages, and abandoned technological ruins. Each region contains characters with unique problems connected to system analysis and design concepts. Players explore the world while managing limited oxygen and discovering hidden routes, diagrams, and spaceship components.

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: The game uses oxygen management, exploration, dialogue interactions, puzzle-solving, and inventory collection as its core mechanics. Players collect spaceship parts, follow system diagrams, and solve educational mini-games related to system analysis and design concepts.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: Limited oxygen creates time pressure, encouraging strategic exploration and faster decision-making. Players learn to interpret diagrams and optimize routes naturally through gameplay. Trial-and-error problem solving and environmental interaction create discovery-driven progression.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: The game aims to create curiosity, tension, achievement, and exploration. Players should feel immersed in an unusual alien world while experiencing satisfaction from solving puzzles and repairing the spaceship step by step. The educational content is designed to feel like part of the adventure rather than traditional learning.

proposalState.introText:
You play as Zyx-7, a nitrogen-breathing alien stranded on Earth after a spaceship crash. Your objective is to repair your spaceship and escape before your oxygen runs out. The game simulates key concepts of System Analysis and Design through interactive exploration and puzzle-solving.

The adventure progresses through 5 main phases:

Help the monkey recover bananas and obtain the system map
Organize the turtle engineer’s workshop using domain modeling
Repair the flower’s water system using user stories and requirements
Design the village fire distribution architecture
Repair and test the spaceship using flowcharts and debugging logic
proposalState.preGameSetup:
Before the game starts, the following systems and configurations must be prepared:

Create the 2D game world, maps, and exploration areas
Configure oxygen meter, inventory system, and dialogue mechanics
Prepare educational puzzles related to system analysis concepts
Assign rewards and spaceship parts to each completed mission
Design tutorial prompts and beginner guidance for first-time players
Test puzzle difficulty balance and progression pacing
Configure sound effects, animations, and user interface elements
Ensure all story events and flowchart interactions trigger correctly
Prepare save/load functionality for player progress tracking

scenarios.name: Main Scenario
scenarios.title: Crash Survival & System Discovery
scenarios.setup: Zyx-7 has crash-landed on Earth. Oxygen levels are decreasing, and the player begins exploration in an unknown environment. The primary goal is survival and obtaining the first system map.
scenarios.objective: Players must complete the first system diagram mission and obtain the first spaceship component.
scenarios.winCondition: Complete the monkey quest + obtain the system map + oxygen ≥ 30%
scenarios.loseCondition: Oxygen = 0 OR time expires due to incorrect decisions
scenarios.storyboard: Players start in a forest after the crash. Oxygen is constantly decreasing, creating time pressure. A monkey requests help. Players must analyze the environment and select correct actions to solve the puzzle. The AI dynamically adapts dialogue and path options based on player choices. After completing the mission, the system analysis map is unlocked, and new objectives become available.
scenarios.algorithm: State-based progression + branching decision tree (Use Case navigation logic)
scenarios.outcome: Banana spawn locations vary each playthrough Monkey hints may differ dynamically Oxygen loss rate increases with incorrect actions

activities.description: The player analyzes the environment to find a way to drop bananas using logic and experimentation.

scenarios.name: Turtle Engineer System Chaos
scenarios.title: Turtle Engineer System Chaos
scenarios.setup: the player enters the second area and encounters a turtle engineer. The workshop is disorganized and system components are mixed incorrectly.
scenarios.objective: Perform domain modeling by correctly classifying objects and obtain the second spaceship component.
scenarios.winCondition: All objects are correctly categorized + motor component acquired
scenarios.loseCondition: Oxygen ≤ 0 OR classification accuracy < 50%
scenarios.storyboard: The turtle engineer asks for help organizing the workshop. Tools, materials, and system components are mixed together. The player must analyze each item and classify it correctly. Incorrect decisions destabilize the system and accelerate oxygen loss, increasing pressure.
scenarios.algorithm: Classification system + rule-based grouping (Domain Modeling logic)
scenarios.outcome: Object sets change each playthrough Some items act as distractors (“trap items”) NPC hints vary dynamically

activities.description: The player analyzes and classifies system elements based on function and type.

proposalState.gameMethod:
Learning is achieved through problem-driven system simulation, where every mechanic represents a real software engineering concept.

Players are placed in situations where they must interpret incomplete or conflicting system information (e.g., NPC requests or broken systems).
To progress, they must apply correct modeling techniques (e.g., classify objects, map processes, design flows).
Oxygen pressure introduces time-constrained decision-making, forcing prioritization similar to real-world system constraints.
Incorrect decisions produce visible system failures, reinforcing causal learning through feedback loops.
Educational content is embedded as functional necessity, not optional information.

proposalState.gameOverview: This proposal presents Island of Systems, a Web Application project targeting COM4061 System Analysis and Design students (3rd/4th year undergraduates, n≈35) in the domain of Education / Learning. The project is grounded in MDA, SDT, Flow Theory, employing Points, Badges, progress, Quests / Missions as primary mechanics.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Players can freely choose which character to help first and how to solve environmental puzzles. Multiple paths, hidden areas, and optional objectives encourage exploration and decision-making. The map system allows players to decide their own strategy while managing limited oxygen resources.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: The game introduces mechanics gradually through simple-to-complex puzzles. Each completed task rewards players with ship parts and new knowledge, creating a clear sense of progress. Oxygen pressure adds challenge without overwhelming the player.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: Players build connections with unique characters such as the monkey, turtle engineer, flower, and villagers by helping solve their problems. Dialogue-driven interactions create emotional attachment and make the player feel important within the world.
proposalState.narrativeEvolution: The story progresses through episodic problem-solving adventures. Early sessions focus on survival and exploration, while later chapters introduce more complex system design challenges and larger-scale puzzles. As players collect spaceship parts and gain knowledge, the world gradually reveals the truth behind Zyx-7’s crash and leads toward the final spaceship repair and escape sequence.
proposalState.socialRationale: Social systems are designed to transform the game from an individual puzzle experience into a collaborative learning environment. By combining competition (leaderboards), cooperation (clubs, mentoring), and communication (forums, feeds), players are encouraged to externalize reasoning, compare strategies, and refine their understanding of system analysis concepts. This structure increases engagement, supports deeper learning, and sustains long-term motivation beyond single-session gameplay.
proposalState.communityStandards:
Players must communicate respectfully in all forums, chats, and collaborative documents. Personal attacks, harassment, discrimination, or toxic behavior are not allowed.

Constructive criticism is encouraged
Disagreement must remain focused on ideas, not individuals
Offensive language or trolling is prohibited
proposalState.adaptiveDifficulty:
The system continuously monitors player performance metrics such as success rate, time spent on puzzles, oxygen efficiency, and number of failed attempts. Based on this data, the AI dynamically adjusts difficulty in real time.

Struggling players are shifted into a support mode, where puzzles are simplified, additional hints are provided, and oxygen depletion is slowed slightly to reduce frustration. High-performing players are shifted into a challenge mode, where puzzle complexity increases, time pressure is intensified, and fewer hints are available to maintain engagement and mastery.

This ensures a balanced experience for all skill levels while maintaining educational effectiveness.
proposalState.personalisation:
The game adapts to each player’s behavior, preferences, and learning style through AI-driven personalization:

Dynamic Hint System: Players receive different levels of guidance depending on their past performance and hesitation patterns.
Route Personalisation: The AI suggests different exploration paths based on player decision history (e.g., logic-oriented vs exploration-oriented routes).
Learning Style Adaptation: Players who perform better in visual tasks receive more diagram-based puzzles, while analytical players receive structured logic challenges.
NPC Interaction Customisation: Dialogue tone and complexity adjust based on player progress and understanding level.

ideaBrief.title: ReqSwipe: Requirements Engineering Challenge
ideaBrief.domain: Software Requirements Engineering (FR vs NFR Classification)

proposalState.organisation: Istanbul Kültür University - Computer Engineering Department
proposalState.domain: Software Requirements Engineering (FR vs NFR Classification)
proposalState.problemStatement: (1) Current situation: Computer engineering students in the COM4061 course often struggle to accurately differentiate between functional system actions and non-functional quality constraints. (2) Identified gap/consequence: Traditional, text-heavy lecture methods lead to low engagement and high misclassification rates, which ultimately cause critical architectural flaws in their final group projects. (3) Proposed gamification intervention: By introducing a rapid, mobile-based swipe mechanic with immediate visual feedback, this project transforms a dry theoretical exercise into a highly engaging, high-velocity learning experience.
proposalState.targetPopulation: Undergraduate Computer Engineering students taking the COM4061 System Analysis & Design course (n=50).
successCriteria: Achievement of ≥ 85% voluntary participation rate (Engagement KPI).
Average classification accuracy improving from 50% baseline to ≥ 90% post-game.
Zero functional/non-functional misclassifications in final term project reports (Transfer KPI).

bloomOutcomes.assessment: Pre-game terminology quiz.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: By the end of the game, participants will be able to clearly explain the fundamental difference between a system action and a quality constraint.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Reviewing static requirement cards and categorizing their core purpose.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Initial baseline accuracy percentage in the warm-up round.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: By the end of the game, participants will be able to independently classify complex requirement statements under simulated time pressure.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Rapid left/right swiping during the high-speed 'Crunch' phase.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Cumulative VP (Velocity Points) and streak multipliers achieved.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: By the end of the game, participants will be able to systematically examine personal error patterns to identify recurring misclassified constraint types.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Reviewing the post-game personal mistake analytics dashboard.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Error-pattern recognition report submitted during the retrospective.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: By the end of the game, participants will be able to critically justify and critique the validity of ambiguous requirement classifications based on IEEE standards.

bloomOutcomes.assessment: Instructor-verified card acceptance rate into the active game database.

proposalState.narrativeTheme: Set in the high-pressure environment of 'LaunchPad 2026', a rapidly growing tech startup, the narrative places players in the role of Lead Software Architects. Their primary adversary is the chaotic, ever-demanding corporate client, 'VaporCorp', which is flooding the team with a massive, unstructured backlog of ambiguous system demands. The core conflict arises as the architects must rapidly triage and classify these demands as Functional or Non-Functional before the startup's funding runway runs out and the system architecture catastrophically fails. To survive this crisis, the architects must act decisively. This high-stakes simulation is deeply grounded in Self-Determination Theory, giving architects the autonomy to manage the backlog. The underlying system utilizes Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics to maintain a state of flow against the ticking clock. Cognitive development is structured according to Bloom Taxonomy, ensuring progression from simple recall to complex architectural evaluation. Furthermore, the environment is tailored to engage diverse player profiles as defined by the HEXAD framework, while the ultimate success of the architect team will be rigorously measured using the Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation to ensure true behavioral transfer.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Players exercise autonomy by independently deciding the classification of ambiguous requirement cards. They choose their own pacing during the review phase and can select which optional 'Hard Mode' sprints to tackle for bonus Velocity Points.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Competence is continuously built and reinforced through the immediate visual feedback of the left/right swipe mechanic. Players see their combo multipliers grow as they master the distinction between functional actions and non-functional constraints.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: The need for relatedness is satisfied through the live session leaderboard where students can compare their sprint times with classmates, as well as the post-game collaborative dashboard where the cohort reviews the most misclassified requirement cards together.

rewardStructure.elite: Top 5% of students on the Velocity Points leaderboard receive the 'Lead Architect' digital badge and a bonus on their final COM4061 project evaluation.
rewardStructure.professional: Students who complete all core sprints with over 90% accuracy earn the 'Senior Developer' title and unlock exclusive avatar customization options.
rewardStructure.access: All participating students who complete the basic onboarding tutorial gain full, unlimited access to their personalized Misconception Dashboard for exam review.

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: The core mechanics of ReqSwipe include rapid left and right swipe gestures to classify requirement cards as Functional or Non-Functional, an adaptive countdown timer that decreases per round, and a streak-based multiplier system for consecutive correct answers.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: These mechanics create a high-speed environment where players experience intense time pressure, forcing them to rely on rapid pattern recognition rather than slow deliberation, leading to split-second decision making and competitive momentum.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: The resulting experience fosters a strong sense of Challenge and Flow, keeping students deeply engaged while building their Competence and mastery over software engineering concepts, ultimately making the learning process feel exhilarating rather than tedious.

scenarios.title: The Core Sprint: Basic Classification
scenarios.setup: The initial phase of the startup simulation where the Lead Architect must rapidly classify a backlog of newly submitted, straightforward client requirements before the funding runs dry.
scenarios.winCondition: Successfully classify at least 15 out of 20 requirement cards with an accuracy rate of 80% or higher.
scenarios.loseCondition: Player fails to classify 15 cards accurately or the timer expires. Under the losePoints=0 compassion model, no penalty points are given to ensure a safe learning environment.
scenarios.outcome: Players earn standard Velocity Points, unlocking the next tier of difficulty and revealing their baseline accuracy.

scenarios.title: The Ambiguity Assessment: Hard Mode
scenarios.setup: The sprint is almost over, but the remaining client demands from VaporCorp are poorly written, highly ambiguous, and mix functional actions with non-functional quality constraints.
scenarios.winCondition: Correctly identify the primary requirement type for 8 out of 10 highly ambiguous statements within the strict 3-minute time limit.
scenarios.loseCondition: Three or more incorrect classifications trigger a strike-out. We employ the losePoints=0 compassion model, so the player simply resets to try again without losing existing progress.
scenarios.outcome: Achieving success grants the player a 2x streak multiplier and the 'Agile Master' temporary badge on the live leaderboard.

scenarios.title: The Retrospective Review: Peer Challenge
scenarios.setup: After the main sprints conclude, players enter the Content Creator Mode where they must evaluate requirement cards designed and submitted by their classmates during the session.
scenarios.winCondition: Accurately validate or reject 5 peer-submitted requirement cards by comparing them against standard IEEE definitions.
scenarios.loseCondition: Failing to correctly validate peer cards results in a timeout. As always, the losePoints=0 compassion model applies, redirecting the student to the Misconception Dashboard instead.
scenarios.outcome: Students receive bonus Velocity Points and their reviewed cards are officially added to the permanent game database for future cohorts.

frs.description: The system shall display randomized software requirement statements from a predefined database for the user to classify.
The system shall register a right-swipe as a 'Non-Functional Requirement' and a left-swipe as a 'Functional Requirement'.
The system shall provide immediate visual and audio feedback (correct/incorrect) instantly after each swipe action.

nfrs.desc: The system shall process swipe gestures and display immediate visual feedback without noticeable delay to maintain the player's Flow state.
nfrs.target: < 200 ms latency per swipe

nfrs.desc: The application interface shall be intuitive enough that first-time students can successfully complete a classification session without needing an external manual.
nfrs.target: ≥ 95% of first-time players play without a tutorial

nfrs.desc: The system shall anonymize all logged player session data, strictly complying with both GDPR and KVKK regulations regarding student data protection.
nfrs.target: 100% compliance with GDPR/KVKK

nfrs.desc: The web application and database must remain highly available during scheduled university laboratory sessions to prevent disruptions during 'The Crunch' phase.
nfrs.target: 99.9% uptime during COM4061 lab hours

nfrs.desc: The backend real-time database (Supabase) shall support simultaneous connections and live score updates for an entire laboratory cohort.
nfrs.target: Supports ≥ 60 concurrent student connections

proposalState.gameDuration: 60 minutes total duration per lab session: 10 minutes onboarding and mechanics tutorial, 30 minutes of high-speed 'Crunch' phase classification sprints, 15 minutes of peer-review content validation, and 5 minutes for final leaderboard reveal and instructor retrospective.

sessionSteps.name: Onboarding & Theory Refresher
sessionSteps.description: Players log in, review the standard IEEE definitions of Functional and Non-Functional requirements, and complete a quick, untimed tutorial to familiarize themselves with the left/right swipe mechanics.

sessionSteps.name: The Crunch Sprints
sessionSteps.description: The core gameplay loop. Students participate in three consecutive high-speed sprints, rapidly classifying requirement cards while managing the adaptive countdown timer to maximize their Velocity Points.

sessionSteps.name: Peer Challenge & Validation
sessionSteps.description: Students transition into Content Creator Mode, where they review, validate, or reject ambiguous requirement statements submitted by their classmates, applying higher-order analytical skills.

sessionSteps.name: Retrospective & Misconception Review
sessionSteps.description: The live session concludes with the final leaderboard reveal. The instructor uses the aggregated Misconception Dashboard to guide the class through the most frequently misclassified cards.

kpis.name: Student Satisfaction and Engagement
kpis.description: Measures COM4061 students' immediate reaction to the ReqSwipe simulation and their perceived utility of the rapid classification mechanics.
kpis.method: Post-session anonymous Likert-scale survey distributed via the CATS LMS platform.
kpis.target: ≥ 85% of participating students rate the simulation as 'Engaging' or 'Highly Engaging'.

kpis.method: Comparative analysis of baseline pre-session scores versus post-session accuracy metrics recorded within the game database immediately after the 60-minute laboratory session.
kpis.target: Average classification accuracy across the student cohort improves from a 50% baseline to ≥ 90% in the final post-session assessment.

kpis.method: Expert audit of the 'System Requirements Specification' section in the final group reports to identify miscategorization errors.

proposalState.logPrivacy: The system shall anonymize all logged player session data, ensuring no personally identifiable information (PII) is stored. All data collection complies with GDPR standards and requires explicit user consent before the session begins.
proposalState.dataCollectionPlan: Quantitative data from the Supabase backend (accuracy and response time) will be analyzed to identify learning gaps. Qualitative survey feedback will be used to refine the UI/UX. The system employs a compassion model, ensuring data is used to support learning, not to punish mistakes.

tuslerRisks.description: Minor visual inconsistencies in the card-swipe animation on specific non-standard mobile screen aspect ratios.
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Implementing a responsive UI container and conducting cross-device testing on the Istanbul Kültür University lab hardware.

tuslerRisks.description: Intermittent network latency during the high-velocity 'Crunch' phase causing slight delays in the next card's appearance.
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Developing a client-side pre-fetching mechanism to load the next five requirement cards in the background.

tuslerRisks.description: A critical database connection failure during the live session's final five minutes, preventing the leaderboard calculation.
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Establishing a redundant database failover and implementing local storage backups for student score data during the session.

tuslerRisks.description: The card pool becomes predictable, leading students to memorize patterns rather than learning the conceptual differences between FR and NFR.
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Integrating a dynamic randomization engine that shuffles distractors and utilizes the 'Content Creator Mode' to constantly refresh the challenge pool.

proposalState.wf_dash: The main dashboard features a high-visibility real-time 'Velocity' counter at the top center, flanked by the current sprint timer and a cumulative accuracy gauge. Below this, a grid layout displays the student's active challenges, pending peer reviews from the Content Creator Mode, and a persistent link to the Misconception Dashboard for rapid error analysis.

To the left, a green 'Functional' drop zone glows upon proximity, while a blue 'Non-Functional' zone persists on the right. A discrete progress bar at the bottom indicates the remaining cards in the active sprint batch.
proposalState.wf_portfolio: The player portfolio screen utilizes a split-pane layout: the left panel shows the user's current 'Lead Architect' rank and earned HEXAD achievement badges, while the right panel displays a detailed historical breakdown of previous sprints. Each entry includes a sparkline of classification accuracy and a list of 'Critical Ambiguities' successfully resolved.
proposalState.whyMotivation: Our pedagogical approach relies on a strict compassion model. By ensuring zero loss and absolutely no punitive measures (losePoints=0) across all gameplay loops, students remain intrinsically motivated to build competence through safe trial and error.

proposalState.introText:
Welcome to DevCEO!
Your objective is to successfully manage and deliver software projects by making correct System Analysis & Design (SA&D) decisions.

This game simulates real-world software project environments where you act as a CEO/project manager. You will analyze requirements, interpret system models, communicate with stakeholders, and choose appropriate system architectures.

The game proceeds through 4 phases:

Message Analysis (Slack/email review)
Decision-Making (selecting actions)
Consequence Simulation (system feedback)
Project Evaluation (score and outcome)

You will have 25–30 minutes to complete a scenario.

Points are earned by:

making correct design decisions
selecting appropriate architectures
managing stakeholder expectations

You succeed when:

your project reaches high satisfaction, profit, and system stability
and avoids critical failures caused by incorrect decisions

proposalState.sdtAutonomy:
Players are provided with meaningful decision-making authority through branching scenarios, where each choice (e.g., selecting system architecture or approving requirements) leads to different consequences.
The system avoids linear progression and instead supports multiple solution paths, reinforcing perceived control over outcomes.
proposalState.sdtCompetence:
The game employs progressive difficulty across three project scenarios (easy → medium → hard), ensuring gradual skill acquisition.
Immediate feedback (e.g., client complaints, project success/failure) and scoring systems reinforce mastery and allow players to learn from mistakes.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness:
Although single-player, the system simulates social interaction through Slack/email communication with stakeholders (clients, team members).
This creates a sense of belonging within a virtual organizational environment, supporting contextual immersion and relational engagement.

Week 4–8: Complexity & Consequences

proposalState.socialRationale:
The system integrates controlled and purpose-driven social interaction to support learning rather than distraction.
Discussion forums allow students to reflect on decisions (e.g., why a requirement was incorrect), enabling asynchronous knowledge exchange.

Collaborative elements (shared analysis notes, peer mentoring) reinforce conceptual understanding through explanation, which aligns with deeper learning strategies.
Leaderboards are restricted and anonymised to prevent discouragement, focusing on self-improvement rather than competition.

The design avoids unnecessary social overload by embedding interaction into learning moments, not as a separate social platform.

proposalState.adaptiveDifficulty:
The system tracks player performance across:

decision accuracy
response time
error patterns

Based on this:

Low-performing players receive simplified scenarios, hints, and reduced complexity
High-performing players face increased difficulty (more variables, tighter constraints, delayed feedback)

This ensures alignment with optimal challenge balance (Flow Theory).

proposalState.projectTitle: The Architects of Avalon: Data Modeling Quest (COM4061 — Single-Player Browser RPG)
proposalState.organisation: Istanbul Kultur University, Department of Computer Engineering
proposalState.deliverableArea: Data Modelling, Entity-Relationship Design, Database Normalisation (1NF/2NF/3NF)
proposalState.problemStatement: Survey of 132 System Analysis and Design students at Istanbul Kultur University showed 71% scored below the threshold on data normalisation tasks; only 29% could correctly identify transitive dependencies in 3NF without scaffolding. Repeated 90-minute lecture interventions across 4 academic terms have not closed this gap, producing measurable competency shortfalls at the Apply and Analyse levels of Bloom's taxonomy.
proposalState.targetPopulation: ≈130 third-year Computer Engineering and Software Engineering undergraduates enrolled in COM4061 across two cohorts (Fall + Spring), aged 20–23
successCriteria: Increase post-test data-modelling accuracy by ≥35% over pre-test baseline within one 45-minute session
Reduce mean error rate on 3NF transitive-dependency tasks from 71% to <25% post-intervention
Achieve ≥80% rubric-graded quality on first post-game ERD submission
≥75% of students retain normalisation rules in a 48-hour delayed quiz

proposalState.narrativeTheme: Avalon Kingdom's Digital Archives are under attack from The Glitch — a chaotic virus that has introduced data redundancy, broken dependencies, and corrupt entity relationships throughout the database infrastructure. The student plays as a Guardian Architect, one of four specialised roles (Scout, Builder, Time-Keeper, Data Sage), tasked with restoring Data Integrity across three increasingly complex domains (University, E-Commerce, Hospital). Each domain is a chapter in the Hero's Journey: Departure (onboarding), Trials (three gameplay bölüms), and Return (portfolio and diagnostic report). The final boss — Redundancy Monster — embodies all three normal form violations simultaneously, and can only be defeated by correctly identifying and eliminating each functional dependency violation in sequence. Theoretical alignment integrates Self-Determination Theory, Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics, Bloom Taxonomy, HEXAD, Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation, Octalysis, and Tusler.
proposalState.gameOverview: The Architects of Avalon is a single-player browser-based RPG that teaches COM4061 data modelling concepts through a fantasy narrative. Students play as Guardian Architects defending Avalon's Digital Archives from The Glitch virus. Across three gameplay bölüms — Entity Discovery, Bridge Building Quest, and Purification Combat — students practise entity identification, primary key selection, cardinality judgement, Junction Table design, and 1NF/2NF/3NF normalisation. The game runs entirely in a single HTML file with no installation required, includes an 8-step tutorial, adaptive difficulty, a drag-and-drop ERD canvas, and a post-session Mastery Radar portfolio. Theoretical alignment integrates Self-Determination Theory, Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics, Bloom Taxonomy, HEXAD, Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation, Octalysis, and Tusler.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Players freely choose their Guardian role (Scout/Builder/Time-Keeper/Data Sage), select the scenario domain (University/E-Commerce/Hospital), set their difficulty level (Easy/Normal/Hard), and decide when to spend Logic Runes in the Grandmaster's Vault — all decisions carry real gameplay consequences, preserving autonomy throughout the 45-minute session
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Visible HP bar, Logic Rune counter, Structural Integrity percentage, and post-session Mastery Radar chart all make competence growth tangible; Scout's Hint scaffold appears after 3 consecutive errors to prevent frustration; adaptive difficulty reduces table complexity when errors accumulate, keeping the challenge in the flow channel
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: Leaderboard with 5 categories (Absolute Score, Sprint, Analytical Contribution, Collaboration, Improvement Rate) fosters peer comparison; 'Challenge a Friend' URL sharing enables informal peer motivation; Instructor Dashboard makes individual progress visible to the instructor-as-mentor, reinforcing the student–instructor relationship

sessionSteps.name: Tutorial Onboarding

sessionSteps.name: Bölüm 1 — Library of Entities
sessionSteps.description: Student selects 3 entities from 6 candidates, assigns Primary Keys, places 9 attributes via drag-and-drop. Guardian's Shield mechanic for PK locking.

sessionSteps.name: Bölüm 2 — Bridge Building Quest
sessionSteps.description: Student selects cardinality type (1:1/1:N/M:N) for 3 entity pairs. M:N triggers Junction Stone placement requiring FK naming.

sessionSteps.name: Bölüm 3 — Purification Combat
sessionSteps.description: Three-phase boss fight: identify 1NF multi-valued column, 2NF partial dependency, 3NF transitive dependency. Each correct answer deals damage to Redundancy Monster.

sessionSteps.name: Portfolio & Diagnostic Review
sessionSteps.description: Mastery Radar chart (5 dimensions), Post-Session Diagnostic Report with Bloom-tagged errors, Kirkpatrick L2 post-quiz, Grandmaster's Vault reward redemption.

proposalState.dataCollectionPlan: Phase 1 (Pre-game): Kirkpatrick L2 pre-quiz results stored in localStorage. Phase 2 (During game): per-answer accuracy logged in session object (concept type, correct/wrong, timestamp, hints used). Phase 3 (Post-game): Kirkpatrick L1 slider ratings, L2 post-quiz, session JSON export available for instructor download. Phase 4 (2 weeks post): Instructor collects ERD assignment rubric scores. Phase 5 (End of term): Exam normalisation-task scores compared with historical cohort. All data collection is opt-in; only aggregated anonymised counts shared with instructor.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Anti-cheat 10-second lockout after each wrong answer eliminates trial-and-error guessing strategy; Scout's Hint mandatory popup after 3 consecutive errors replaces guessing with scaffolded explanation
Auto-save to localStorage after every action with Resume Banner on reload; player advised to export JSON at session end; addresses Wi-Fi disruption and shared-lab machine risk
Fully static single-file HTML deployment; no server dependency; CDN-hosted fonts with local fallbacks; offline-capable once downloaded — eliminates server-outage risk
WCAG AA colour contrast verified (≥4.5:1 for all text); keyboard navigation supported; mobile-responsive layout; touch support on ERD canvas via pointer-event polyfill
8-step tutorial with skip option; Glossary overlay with 20 defined terms accessible via floating button at all times; keyboard shortcut help tooltip displayed once on first session
Adaptive difficulty reduces table complexity after 2+ consecutive normalisation errors, keeping challenge within the flow channel and preventing learned helplessness
Scenario originality is >35% different from all 22 GPPT worked examples (different narrative theme, mechanics, and domain vocabulary); automatic originality check in GPPT export

Compassion model: losePoints=0 scoring; no punitive point deductions; failure triggers Scout's Hint scaffold instead of punishment.
